sentence_text: This is the day we get down to work.
Yesterday, the guideline panel sat through lectures on guideline methodology, debated the quality rating criteria for the individual studies, and approved the recommendation grading system.
My job doesn't have an official title.
As a general internist, I am almost never a clinical expert on the panels I facilitate.
I am chosen for my guideline methods experience.
Part guide, part policeman, I support the panel members through the process, acting as a resource for the finer procedural points and bringing them back to the evidence when they stray.
This panel is like many others.
The quipster across from me has probably been making jokes since kindergarten.
The quiet one at the end—I take note of her.
She may have some important points, but the group will need to be stilled to hear them.
I try to measure up the chair.
Can he participate but limit his own desire to join in?
Will he draw out his colleagues' expertise?
Will he help me when it's time to move on?
I have learned over the years that panel members often have wells of knowledge and talent that they sometimes don't share.
It's my job to engage them.
We get started.
We tackle the easy question first.
There is a large body of good-quality evidence, which I present to the group.
Slowly, hesitantly, the panel members wade through the evidence tables.
I allow the debate to move freely—this is necessary.
When some people start looking bored, I move the panel to a close.
It's time to write the recommendation.
This panel is particularly good; everyone participates.
The recommendation sounds terrible at first—too long, too many words, unclear.
Who knew it was so hard to write a simple sentence?
The panel is discouraged.
I volunteer to put up a sentence.
They are happy to tackle it, because I am neither friend nor colleague.
They play with the words.
Should or must?
Smokers, people who smoke, or tobacco users?
Patients or clients?
Willing to quit, attempting to quit, or making a quit attempt?
Readers of guidelines often dismiss the recommendations as poorly written, not recognizing the effort the panel members give to crafting the best sentence possible.
I know how hard panel members try.
When they hesitate, bogged down by the rules, I challenge them and remind them of their job: They are here to provide guidance.
What does the panel really want to say?
I play the part of the nonexpert clinician.
What do I want to hear?
I urge them to write something as specific and unambiguous as the evidence will allow.
We finish the sentence and vote on it.
This panel decided that 80% approval was required to move to the next part; we have unanimous support.
We then vote on the level of evidence.
The strength of the recommendation?
“Strong,” the panel members decide.
The first recommendation is complete.
Everyone is pleased and relieved.
It has taken half the day.
Midway through lunch, we begin again.
In the afternoon, panel members, more comfortable with the process, take over from me and present the evidence to their colleagues.
They become even more engaged.
They have the hang of it now, and the process moves quickly.
Soon after, we lose track of time, and the recommendations flow of their own accord.
I love this part.
We finish the afternoon with 5 recommendations to our credit.
The guideline is but half complete.
Many teleconferences will follow, but the bonds have been forged, the training has been assimilated, and the group departs content with a job well-done.
I make my way home, exhausted.
The first time my father took me to see an orchestra, I asked him who the man at the front was.
“The conductor,” he said.
“But he's not playing an instrument.
Why is he there?
He just seems to be waving his hands all the time,” I said.
“Don't be fooled.
The conductor plays a very important part.
He brings the orchestra together.
See, his movements have a purpose.
Right now he is guiding the violins.”
Not playing an instrument myself, but wanting to be part of the music, I considered my father's words.
I could do that one day, I thought
The sirens blared, and the flashing lights painted the road red as ambulance 43 weaved its boxy way to the Denver county jail.
My partner and I threw open the back doors and wheeled out the bright white bed that glowed in the dark silence that always seems to surround prisons, like lips tightly sealed around a secret.
As we prepared to walk into the front of the jail, I caught a glimpse of the full moon filling the circumference of one of the wheels of razor wire.
It felt like we were being watched by a giant spotlight surveying the grounds.
We pushed through the entrance and gave the guard a nod.
They were ready for us.
The metal detector cried out when my stethoscope violated its invisible border, as if it knew we were here to take away one of its coveted possessions.
As we navigated the long, narrow hallways, the rusty wheels of the bed put up only a half-hearted fight, like an aged boxer going through the motions of just one more round.
It knew these halls well.
Blank stares came from the tiny cells around us; our green scrubs fantastically contrasting with their blue ones, as if we were in some deep-sea exhibit.

Our entourage of police and paramedics found the girl in the infirmary, which looked like it was straight out of a Hitchcock film—archaic medical devices lined up against the walls, contorting our faces in their cold, metallic reflections.
Handcuffs swallowed the prisoner's wrists and ankles as the guards fixed her into a position that resembled a spider sticking to its web.
She was quiet and still.

The Eastern European guard— bound by the rigid rules of his paperwork—was reluctant to turn the patient over to us without first filling in his line marked for complications: “None” was an unacceptable option to him.
Apparently, a 7-pound object ready to burst out of the prisoner was not enough.
My stethoscope scanned her body, searching for any signs of abnormality.
I briefly thought of the irony of the term “HELLP syndrome” before I told the guard that the prisoner seemed stable.
He suspiciously smiled his jaundiced teeth at her and let us go, but not before reminding us that “those cuffs are going to have to stay on for the entire delivery.”
The police officer with us mumbled something under his breath, and we whisked the patient back down the same narrow hallway, past the same blank stares.
“Social Work will meet us at L&D to take the baby into custody after the delivery,” the paramedic instructed.
“Oh, and please don't push yet.”
After we loaded the prisoner into the back of the ambulance, along with the pair of armed police officers, the prisoner and I made eye contact for the first time.
What I saw surprised me.
Her eyes were filled with sorrow and loneliness—a look completely unlike what I had become used to seeing on the labor and delivery deck.
I knew nothing about this woman's past, but it didn't matter.
She was a soon-to-be mother, plagued by the knowledge that her firstborn child would be taken from her.
It seemed like a plot line out of a Greek tragedy, and I had become a part of it.
Not once in those 20 minutes inside the jail did it occur to me to introduce myself.
It was at that moment that something else began to bother me.
I debated whether what I was about to ask would be laughed off, or if it would be viewed as another naive question from a medical student along for his first ambulance ride.
I didn't want them to think it was a joke, because it wasn't.
I decided to risk it and turned back toward the paramedic.
“How do you deliver a baby when your legs are handcuffed to the bed?”
The paramedic paused for a second, shrugged her shoulders and said, “I always try and leave before that part.”
“I have a very big favor to ask you.”
Most members of my synagogue had heard, at one time or another, the unmistakable voice of Rachel Rosen at the other end of the phone.

For almost any organized activity—a Sunday school picnic, a charity drive, a reception for a new family, an evening lecture by a visiting scholar—“Rachel the Arranger” could be found working behind the scenes, making sure the event went off perfectly.
I immediately began to compose my regrets.
This was not a good time for big favors.
My children were out of school for the summer, my wife was increasingly busy with her new practice, and I had a big grant due at the end of the month.

“I don't know if you've heard the bad news,” she warned, and then proceeded to tell me that one of our congregants, who was also one of my colleagues at the Medical Center, had tragically died of a heart attack while jogging earlier that day.
“I'm lining up Shomerim for tonight; I hope you can help.”
Jewish tradition dictates that the body of the deceased is never left alone from the moment of death until it is laid to rest in the earth.
Pious members of the congregation wash the body; dress it in a simple white shroud; and place it in a closed wooden casket.
Then Shomerim—watchmen—stand a constant vigil through the night, praying and reading psalms to comfort and honor its soul.
But these customs are not usually practiced in the Reform Jewish tradition in which I was brought up.
Yet, I had unknowingly stood as a Shomer once before.
It was Christmas Eve of my intern year, and I had volunteered to take call.
Preholiday discharges had emptied the hospital of all but the critically ill, the sickest of whom was Mildred Rubenstein, an elderly woman who had suffered a massive heart attack and was dying.
“Has anyone notified the family?”
They had: Her only son was flying in from California, but an ice storm had delayed all of the flights into Chicago.
I scribbled some notes in her chart and then retired to the call room.
My goal was not to leave it for anything except the most urgent emergencies until 6:00 a.m., when my shift ended.
I was soon deep asleep.
Sometime soon thereafter, the phone rang.
Mrs. Rubenstein had died, and the charge nurse wanted me to come up to pronounce her death.
“Can't this wait until morning?”
“No, it can't.”
Disgruntled, I trudged back to the CCU.
I verified absent vital signs, filled out the requisite paperwork, and watched the nurses perform their final rituals and wheel the body away.
I then hastened back to the sanctuary of the call room and sleep.
The phone rang again around 4:00 a.m.
Mrs. Rubenstein's son had arrived.
The nurses had informed him of his mother's passing, but he was asking to see me.
“What does he want?”
“He wants to see the doctor.”
Once again I returned to the CCU.
Mrs. Rubenstein's son was seated at the conference room table.
He was still wearing his trench coat, his hair was soaked from the storm, and a deep sadness lay in his eyes.
“I'm so sorry about your mother,” I said.
“Can I see her?”
“They've already taken her downstairs.”
I want to see her one last time.”
One last time . .
by Jewish tradition, her casket would be closed.
This was his last chance to visit with his mother.
I called a security officer, who met us in the basement and unlocked the morgue.
Inside, I was dismayed to find 10 enshrouded cadavers on gurneys crammed into the walk-in refrigerator.
I circled around them, searching for any identifying feature, but not even a toe tag was visible.
Tentatively, I unwrapped the most promising candidate; it was not Mrs. Rubenstein.
I found her on the fourth try.
I wiped the secretions from around her mouth and straightened out her hair as best I could, then wheeled the gurney into an empty freight elevator, the only place that offered any privacy.
“You can come in now,” I announced, and retreated a respectful distance.
I heard her son speaking softly to her and then heard sobbing.
After some time he emerged.
“Thank you so much, Doctor,” he said, taking my hand.
But he made no move to leave.
“We should go back now,” I said.
Could I ask you for one more favor?
Would you stay with my mother until the funeral director arrives?”
What a strange request, I thought to myself.
and I'm tired and I just want to go back to sleep.
But still, something finally moved me to assent.
“Go get some rest,” I told him at last.
“I'll stay here with your mother.”
The security guard escorted him away, I pulled up a chair, and once again I was alone with Mrs. Rubenstein.
Exhausted, I fell into a fitful dream: I began to hear chanting in Hebrew, I could make out people standing around a grave—I was at my sister's funeral.
Most of the faces were blurred, but one stood out with startling clarity.
It was Dr. Anya Petrovsky, the pediatric oncologist who had cared for my sister throughout her illness.
A large, imposing Russian woman, she was known among the housestaff as “The Commissar.”
My sister—one of the few who could evoke her gentleness—had called her Dr. Anna.
Silent tears were streaming down her face.
She was attending her patient for the last time.
I jolted awake, disoriented and strangely disturbed.
After a few moments, I did the most peculiar thing: I started talking to Mrs. Rubenstein about my sister.
“She was only fourteen years old.
You know, she had been doing really well, but then she started getting so weak.
They put her in Children's Hospital and I visited her every night.
I'd bring her ice cream and hold her hand, and we'd talk about my college life.
One night there was a big dance on campus, but I didn't want to leave her. 'Go have some fun,' she said.
'You can tell me all about in the morning.'
So I went and stayed out all night.
But when I went back to the hospital early the next morning, her room was empty.
'Where's my sister?”
I asked a nurse.
Oh, I'm so sorry.
She died last night.
We couldn't reach you.
They've taken her to the funeral home.'”
“I wasn't there when she died,” I wept.
“I never saw her again...I never said goodbye.
She didn't look like she was dying.
She didn't seem like she was afraid.
In fact, that night she looked so... radiant.”
And as I poured out my heart to Mrs. Rubenstein, I hoped that someone had stood watch over my sister, too.
The funeral director arrived at 6:00 a.m. I climbed back up the stairs into the light of the wards, checked out my patients, and stepped out into the cold morning air.
The ice storm had passed, and the sky was a brilliant electric blue of such intensity that it seemed to touch the earth.
The trees were glazed with a thick crystal coating that refracted the morning sun into dazzling flashes of color.
I felt a profound sense of peace that I would not fully understand until much later.
Rachel's voice returned my thoughts to the present.
“I'm sorry to ask on such short notice, but I really need someone for the late night shift.”

And as I held the phone, I recalled the final request of Mrs. Rubenstein's son, I saw the crisp white sheets on the empty bed in my sister's hospital room, and I felt a shard of the pain that I knew my late colleague's family was now suffering.

In his Daily Prayer of a Physician, Maimonides gives thanks to God that “Thou hast chosen me in thy mercy to watch over the life and death of thy creatures”—but watching over death is the hardest thing a physician must do, for it seems to be the antithesis of all that we are.
Yet, as I have confronted death in the many years that have passed since that icy night, I have seen how the seemingly smallest gestures often provide the most immense comfort.
Paradoxically, it is perhaps in those moments that we have the greatest power to heal.
“When do you need someone?”
I asked Rachel.
“From 4 o'clock until 6 in the morning.”
This time, I did not hesitate.
“I'll be there.”
Stepping off the plane in an unfamiliar country, jetlagged, and unable to speak a word of the native language, I was a little disoriented.
I met my patient—whom I will call Rashid—at the hotel in less than an hour of my arrival.
He was apprehensive.
We spent the evening in a relaxed, informal atmosphere with a group of attorneys.
Despite the fact that I was a white, non-Muslim, American woman, we were able to make a connection.

Rashid took us to a beautiful beach, peppered with traditional wooden fishing boats, and he gave local children a few coins to pick some baobab fruit for us to sample from a very unusual looking tree.
The attorneys, Rashid, and I listened to his music and teased him about his taste in Western hip-hop.
As a physician evaluator for Physicians for Human Rights, I had been asked to interview Rashid.
There was no clinical space in which to perform this evaluation, so we used my hotel room.
I quickly learned the extent of Rashid's profound suffering.
He described himself as a ghost wandering around the town in isolation, unable to eat or sleep.
He repeatedly asked me if he was “crazy,” because that is how he perceived himself.
He detailed the horrors of his arrest, during which he was beaten so badly that he was admitted to a hospital with multiple fractures and internal injuries.
He described how he was kidnapped from his hospital bed and survived a 5-year ordeal in U.S. custody in multiple detention sites, including Afghanistan.

In the stifling, humid hotel room, he detailed the methods of his torture: severe beatings, prolonged painful stress positions, prolonged solitary confinement, forced nakedness and humiliation, sleep deprivation, withholding of food, sexual assault (anal rape and sodomy), forced intravenous medication during interrogations that he thought might be a “truth serum,” and painful shackling.
At times, he was denied medical and psychiatric care.
As the hours wore on, Rashid's story became almost unbearable.
There were times when we both broke down in tears.

Rashid described being forced to lie on a wet mat, naked and handcuffed, having cold water poured on him while being kicked and beaten, and then rolled up in the wet mat “like a corpse”—cold, wet, and shivering.
In the “water room,” men attempted to insert the spout of a water jug into his anus.
He reported that his arms were chained to an overhead pipe while he was in a standing position for what he estimates was 4 days.
His heels could not touch the floor and he developed severe back and shoulder pain.
He was not allowed to use the toilet, and loud music blared the entire time.
(“It is just death.”)
He went on to describe being locked naked in a “coffin”; he could not move and it was difficult to breathe.
At one point during his detention, his flesh started rotting under a cast that was left unattended for too long.
His request for medical care was ignored until he protested by going on a hunger strike for 6 days.

Rashid's prominent symptoms included extreme sleep disturbance, sadness, loss of appetite with substantial weight loss, and difficulty interacting with other people, (including family and friends), resulting in profound isolation.
He has not been able to work as a result of his debilitating psychological symptoms and has no means to support himself.
The lack of self-sufficiency has caused further depression, feelings of inadequacy, and shame and humiliation when he has to rely on his family for basic needs.
Rashid told me he wakes up at 2 a.m. and takes walks.
(“My head feels empty, like an empty box.”)
His life has unraveled since his return from U.S. custody, and he is unable to return to his former level of functioning and reintegrate back into his family and community.
He meets diagnostic criteria for posttraumatic stress disorder and major depression, but those Western-based diagnoses do not adequately characterize his palpable suffering.
In the evening, Rashid borrowed a car and we decompressed on a drive to the beautiful bush and beaches.
I had to abandon the Western doctor–patient boundaries.
I learned about local life and customs and tasted the local food.
I photographed the old colonial buildings and monkeys and camels.
I then met his mother and sister, at his request.
We sat on the floor of a simple but welcoming cement structure, were served sweets and coffee, and were treated as warmly as if we were family or close friends.
I explained to Rashid's family that he wasn't “crazy” and that there was hope for recovery.
Neither Rashid nor his family knew of posttraumatic stress disorder, or understood the link between the torture and his symptoms.
Their gratitude for my simple explanation was humbling.
We conducted our final interviews on the beach at Rashid's request.
After his 5 years in confinement, this vast expanse of beauty was a place of solace and comfort for him.
He would stop every so often and illustrate a point by drawing in the sand, such as when he drew a rough reproduction of a cage in which he had been confined.
It was difficult to leave Rashid after having uncovered such desperate need and having no available resources to alleviate his suffering.
My fears were intensified by the fact that Rashid's suffering was not the aftermath of a natural disaster, but one deliberately inflicted by other humans.
After departure, I received a text message from Rashid.
“Thank u my Dr. U help me very much and my brain it gonna be OK.”
This unique and complicated situation demanded creative and unorthodox measures.
The combined collaboration of Physicians for Human Rights and a local organization made initial treatment for this man a reality.

After I left, the United Nations Voluntary Fund for Victims of Torture agreed to fund treatment and rehabilitation services, and a psychologist traveled from a neighboring country try to provide mental health treatment.
An antidepressant was sent by post, and I initially gave instructions and monitored Rashid's progress by cell phone.
I continue to have regular phone contact with him to provide support and to monitor symptoms while trying to organize more regular treatment.
I have seen improvement in his self-esteem because he is better able to understand the root cause of his symptoms.
I have detected something new in our communication— hope.
Rashid is now hopeful about his recovery and future.
And I am hopeful that it is possible to repair the wounds my country has inflicted.
Rashid was given a document upon his release that confirmed his confinement as well as his innocence.
On the basis of my evaluation, I have a high degree of medical certainty that he was tortured while in U.S. custody.
Nonetheless, neither he nor I know the identity of the perpetrators.
I studied undergraduate medicine at a university in Orissa, one of the eastern states of India.
In this primarily rural, paddy-crop state, half of the population lives below the poverty line, with a yearly per capita income equal to about $100.
This is one of the most impoverished states in India.
Therefore, as an intern, I rotated in an extremely poorly funded state hospital.
Unlike other state facilities in the country, the only thing that we could provide free to our patients was medical consultation.
We had neither adequate supplies of medication in our pharmacy nor enough equipment in our inventory.
Admitted patients had to purchase medications required for their treatment from expensive, privately owned stores outside the hospital.
Sometimes we had to ask patients to buy such essentials as swabs, dressings, intravenous catheters, and disposable syringes as well.
Not surprisingly, our hospital was a refuge for only the critically ill among the critically poor.
People with better means preferred private nursing homes, and understandably so.
On one of those unforgiving, blazing subtropical afternoons during my internship, I was on call on the general medicine floors.
A lanky, dark, middle-aged fellow with a sunburned, unshaven face trudged across the hallway.
His threadbare vest was so drenched in sweat that it seemed as if it would start dripping any moment.
He was carrying a frail-looking boy in his arms—for the lack of a stretcher, I presumed.
The boy looked very young, although I guessed he must have been at least in his late teens or he would have been admitted to pediatric floors.
He was drowsy and breathing fast and shallow, moaning occasionally.
I was told that this young boy was my new patient.
The nurses laid the boy on one of the beds, and I measured his vitals.
He was hypotensive, with systolic blood pressure palpable at around 80 mm
Hg and a heart rate in the 120s.
Normal saline boluses started in the emergency room were still going.
His skin must have been as hot as the cement veranda outside; the thermometer read 104 °F.
I reviewed his chart notes.
The senior physicians suspected septic shock due to pneumonia.
An accompanying chest film showed some lobar consolidation.
“Your son?” a nurse questioned.
I couldn't tell if the man nodded or sighed.
It was a particularly busy time at the hospital.
We had many cases of cerebral malaria, our intensive care beds were full, and telemetry monitors were unthinkable outside of the intensive care unit.
One of the nurses asked the boy's father why he had not taken him to a nursing home.
“What do you think, sister?” replied the father, wiping his forehead.
“I carried my boy on my back and walked 3 miles to reach the hospital because I didn't have 5 rupees for the bus.”
I did not know back then that 5 rupees amounted to 10 cents.
I looked at the father.
The wrinkles around his eyes looked like little crevasses.
His face bore an expression I could not understand.
There were shades of sorrow, but more than that, an air of resignation that frightened me.
I would be lying if I said that this was the poorest patient we had ever admitted, or that we tried to help each of our patients without means.
However, on that day it happened.
It was clear to us that the boy's father could not purchase medications, so we asked the attending physician to arrange for some supplies.
He was able to borrow antibiotics and a vial of phenylephrine from the surgical floor.
The remaining staff and I collected some money for syringes, intravenous catheters, and other basic provisions, and we began treatment.
Throughout the night, we administered boluses of normal saline and hoped that he would not require high doses of phenylephrine, because the small vial was all we had.
All night, his blood pressure hovered precariously between low and low-normal, his heart rate and temperature stayed high, and the pulse oximeter reading remained borderline.
The father put cold compresses on his son's body.
The boy remained semiconscious, fidgeting with his oxygen mask and complaining to his father.
At dawn, for the first time in 16 hours, the boy opened his eyes and looked at his father.
His temperature plummeted, and his blood pressure rose to 3 digits.
The night nurse squeezed my hand in sheer joy, and the father wept.
Over the next 2 days, the boy improved considerably.
His father stayed with him; slept on the floor by his bed; and prayed, squatting, next to the door.
He set up a small kitchen in the corridor close to his son's station and regularly cooked fresh lentil soup and rice for him.
On the sixth day, when we were ready to discharge the boy, the father came up to me holding a small gunnysack.
He sat on the floor, put the sack at my feet, joined his palms together, and shyly explained that he had brought some rice.
I looked at him questioningly.
He added almost apologetically that a little rice was all he had to show his gratitude.
With whatever local language I could muster, I told him that it was not necessary.
I explained that the government paid me to do my job.
But he would not budge.
He said it was a gift.
I told him that doctors could not accept gifts from patients, yet he would not leave until I accepted the little burlap bag.
I often remember this incident, especially whenever there are discussions about physicians accepting gifts from their patients.
I know that position papers on medical ethics say that small gifts from patients are not ethically problematic.
However, 6 pounds of rice from an indigent farmer hailing from an Indian state of famine and drought was not a small gift.
That bag of rice has been the only gift I have ever accepted from a patient.
It has also been one of the most expensive things I have ever had the pleasure of holding in my hands.
I donated it to the hospital kitchen later, and have never questioned the ethics of the matter.

I n his famous story, “The Emperor's New Clothes,” Danish author Hans Christian Andersen tells of an emperor, vain and pompous, who is scammed by rogues who tell him that that they can weave him royal regalia made of a cloth so exquisite and fine that no one can see it but the most competent and intelligent of persons.
When the scoundrels mime draping him in this miraculous fabric, he stands naked, but no one—including the emperor himself—dare say so, for fear that they will be thought to be stupid and inept.
It isn't until the Emperor parades proudly through the streets unclothed that a child cries out (the only person to do so in a large crowd of cheering subjects) that the emperor is naked.

Recently, as an attending physician on an internal medicine housestaff inpatient teaching service, I saw a middle-aged woman who had been transferred to us from the orthopedics floor after yet another in a series of episodes of septic arthritis.

She smiled at me when, between short periodic bouts of weeping and writhing, she told me that she had had “over 40 operations” on the currently affected knee alone, as well as at least 20 surgeries on other joints and various other parts of her body.
She had total body pain, she said, and had spent much of the last 20 to 30 years of her life being recurrently hospitalized and operated on.
“Why do you think you have had so much trouble?”
I asked her.
“I have Ehlers–Danlos,” she answered.
“I guess I'm just susceptible.
That's what the doctors told me.”

She had no family history of hypermobility syndromes nor, on my examination, did she have any evident physical findings of Ehlers–Danlos syndrome variants (which are all due to genetically variable defective connective tissue) and generally a clinical diagnosis.
It was impossible for me to test even the few remaining unoperated joints of her hands and feet for hypermobility, as she cried out in pain at the merest touch.
Her skin was not velvety, nor was it abnormally stretchable.
There was no bruising of her skin, and her multiple surgical scars seemed to have healed normally; they were not wide or nodular.
Her hard palate was not high arched, and her feet were not flat.
Did she indeed have Ehlers–Danlos syndrome?

Indeed, every note previously written in the electronic medical record (EMR) throughout her multiple admissions recorded it as her history and included it in her long list of final diagnoses, but not a single one of these notes detailed any aspects of physical examination that suggested that those who wrote them (attendings, residents, fellows, medical students, or nurses) had actually looked for it.

Many of these physical examination notes, both initial and daily follow-up, which were written during any one of her many hospitalizations, were identical, almost word for word (except for the specific joint in peril at the time), although they were supposedly written by different people on different days.
It seems that no one at any level of expertise, at least as was recorded in our EMR, had ever seriously questioned the diagnosis of Ehlers–Danlos syndrome.

There was one distant note by a medical resident who saw her on an emergency department visit that added something new in social history, which was that the patient had begun training to be a nurse but never graduated.
Was he thinking, as I was, of Munchausen syndrome?
If so, why did he not specify the positive and negative physical findings for Ehlers–Danlos syndrome, the underlying disease that she claimed was the root source of her problems?
My third-year medical student assigned to this patient gave me her written admission workup to critique, which I did.
There it was again—“Ehlers–Danlos”—both in the history and in the diagnosis list, but not in physical examination.
“Tell me about the Ehlers–Danlos syndromes,” I asked her.
She had done her reading and rattled off a highly competent summary of what she had learned.
“What makes you think this patient has an Ehlers– Danlos syndrome?”
You've just told me—and very well—the characteristic findings on PE, but they appear nowhere, either as positives or negatives, in your physical examination.
She seemed a bit stunned by my question.
“I looked for them, but I couldn't see them” she answered.
“Then why did you say she had it?”
“You know, I wondered about it, but everybody else seemed to think she had it.”
She threw up her hands and stomped her foot, angry at herself.
“I know that I should have said something!
But I'm just a student, and I didn't want to seem stupid.”
The EMR is a wonderful but dangerous tool.

Its capacity for efficient redundancy or, more perniciously, overt and unedited cut-and-paste from previous notes, allows doctors—either pressed for time or uncertain of themselves (as even very good doctors continually are)—to simply replicate their previous notes or parrot the antecedent notes of another clinician.
This poses an increasingly particular danger for medical students, who tend to believe that everybody else must know more than they do.
Yet, it is a hazard not just for students, but for all of us.

Uncritical acceptance of what others have said is not a new phenomenon in medicine, but it has been much facilitated by the EMR, a real-world equivalent of the clever huckster weavers of Andersen's story.

His was a cautionary tale, and we should take a lesson from it: It is only by using our own senses and minds and taking the time to record only our own accurate findings and thoughts—and having the ethical imperative and courage to do so—that we can serve our patients well.

Although I spoke to my team and wrote in my EMR attending notes of my doubts, recording my physical examinations as specifically showing no current findings for Ehlers–Danlos and suggesting a possible alternative diagnosis of Munchausen syndrome, I saw no major change of content in a large number of the subsequent EMR notes by a variety of other writers.
Maybe, unlike the child at the emperor's parade in Hans Christian Andersen's story, I did not cry out loudly enough to be heard by the crowd.
on any color coat, and folks will call you “doc.”
Every time they said it, I breathed a quick reminder that I wasn't a doctor, as of yet.
Still it felt like a game—putting on my cloak of illusion and temporarily stepping into a different world.
But, like many others, I was experiencing things that would be important in either helping me to actually get there or sending me running in the other direction.
Both receiving and giving training, after all, is a big part of being a doctor.
If you ever thought that career shadowing was a worthless exercise in babysitting, I assure you that nothing could be further from the truth.
I am a blank canvas, a dry sponge, the timeless tabula rasa.
Paint me a picture, let me soak up your wisdom, and I'll understand.
My perspective is a unique one: I'm not a doctor, but I've worked under quite a few of them, and they speak openly with me.
How many nonphysicians get unguarded insight into medicine?
Yet, with each iteration, I got the same question: Why do you want to go into medicine?
It used to hit like a cruel joke half of the time when I sensed that what they meant was: “Why would you?”
The question doesn't bother me now—after dozens of such encounters I've settled on an acceptable answer.
But into the stunned silence of earlier trials, my hosts would often impart their responses.
Concerned physicians stepped in to ask the incoming generations what we were thinking.
Is there a reason to it all, or are we just playing doctor?
Are we looking for the challenge, the pursuit and mastery of difficult skills and knowledge?
Most of us who find ourselves signing on for another 4 years of postbaccalaureate education can hardly be called slackers.
Medicine has been an obvious destination for bright minds for ages (admissions criteria prove this).
And the challenge cannot be overstated—the work is often stressful and risky, and the hours can grow long.
No one has lied to the incoming generation of medical students and told them to expect a cake walk.
But the road doesn't seem to be quite as rocky as it once was.
Many medical schools have hearkened to the cries of the student body by instituting reasonable curriculum changes, or reducing the amount of in-class time.
Councils of professionals and some employers are striving to decrease the hours spent on the job or on call during residency.
New technologies have taken some of the guesswork away.
Admittedly, new headaches have been added in some cases.
From my experience, many veteran physicians (rightly) comport themselves as a battle-hardened elite and lecture stereotypically on how comparatively easy I'll have it.
Yet I didn't sign on because I wanted to run a course that was uphill both ways.
Are we sniffing around for the money or the lifestyle?
Some from every generation have, but fiscal revelations aren't going to blindside my class.
No one gets to medical school without talking to a doctor, and I don't know any doctors who have told folks to do it for the paycheck.
I've met a few who are unhappy with their pay, some who don't think about it, and some who admit to enjoying the comfort.
My nontraditional classmates left positions in consulting, investment banking, and other lucrative fields for medicine, and most other people who make up my class can understand why.
Remarks to help orient incoming students this year may only have served to reinforce the notion that health care as we know it might undergo serious change by the time we're ready to practice.
We've witnessed recent legislative battles over reimbursement.
Are we a special class of risk takers, gambling on the future of medicine?
No, I and most others would not rely solely on such fragile guarantees.
Do we crave respect and a sense of power?
The “Dr.” honorific has been a self-empowering status symbol for centuries.
But the silent testimony of a wearied, haggard resident with fresh vomit on his scrub bottoms highlights that sometimes it's rather hard to feel respected.
You can tell a person to master delayed gratification, but few would be willing to endure 4-plus years of such training simply for the respect of crossing the finish line.
More accurately, many of us struggle to avoid the rote tedium and lack of individual significance we've found elsewhere.
Have we all been swept up in the idea that we simply want to help people?
This would not relegate us to becoming physicians, or even to medicine, but the idea of direct action is appealing to some.
For those who have grown up in a world that keeps people at arm's length, increasingly impersonal and detached, medicine still permits (and requires) intimate contact and connection.
If we didn't grow up with toy stethoscopes and white coats, we still found our way to them eventually as we turned from other paths without as much heart.
These were some of the experiences shared throughout interview season, and for many, they are a sort of premedical raison d'eˆtre.
Although some sounded good to me and to admissions boards across the country, none of these are my answer.

It was dumb luck that helped me one day as I found myself in an off-white, tiled operating room, standing scared in the corner when a gruff, rather annoyedlooking orthopedic surgeon walked in with his damp forearms raised as if to ward me off.
Looking back, the operation was unimportant, largely because I had to play a game of finding a clean line of sight through the jumble of anesthesia machines and medical personnel.
But after an hour on my feet the blood must have rushed from my head, because I was all of a sudden brave enough or brash enough to ask the surgeon what was going on.
He started to give me a look that could have killed, but that would probably have made more work for him.
Instead, he spat out at me, “This bone is really broken.
I'm going to fix it.”
Health care is really broken.
Each successive generation of physicians is “going to fix it.”
Some are going to provide that personal touch that's being taken away.
Some are going to accept careers that demonstrate care for the greater community and a responsibility for the general welfare.
Some are going to realize that they still have energy after the rigors of today's training to begin to tackle problems in policy or research.
At some level, I'm going to take a long look at what needs to change and I'm going to design a solution.
I'm going to fix it.
I'm not a doctor yet, but I've soaked up a little bit from quite a few.
Thanks are due to every one of them who shared his or her time, not just with me but with any inquiring young mind.
To some, it meant giving up valuable time and interrupting a routine, but it was an investment that will pay dividends to our entire society.
The next time you have a chance to be that mentor, don't hesitate.
I'm not afraid of learning from texts, but they aren't a complete source.
Keep asking me why it is I want to be a doctor until I am one, and maybe I'll never forget.
What does it feel like?
Stop for a moment and count the stones in front of your feet.
The stones are always there.
Sometimes it's hard to see their shape or color, but if you gaze long enough, they become clear, distilled beneath the cold water of the childhood spring that carries away your unsteady reflection.
Reach 56 and wait.
Don't go any further, do not succumb to the temptation to keep counting—just wait ... .
I came to Yoram's house in the early afternoon of a hot, sunny day in the middle of May—a small country house, neat garden, a couple of rather friendly dogs.

The wooden front door was open, unveiling a pungent smell of that unique nature that not only tells you with fingerprint accuracy what family it belongs to but also their ages, loves, hesitations, and the exact amount of salt they put in the chicken soup, and in tears.
This time I could detect mashed potatoes, orange zest, 2-day-old bread, wet dust, and a thin scent of sadness.
Then again, that morning it was my scent as well, and the one thing they don't teach you in medical school is to distinguish your own sadness from others'.
“Hello, Dr. Limor.”
Dalia, Yoram's wife, came from the kitchen wiping her hands on a plaid IKEA towel.
“So nice of you to come.
He is expecting you.
They called from the hospital earlier and asked him if the hospice doctor had come to visit him.
Would you believe the audacity of these people?
Fortunately, he doesn't know the meaning of this word, and a good thing he doesn't have the patience to look it up.
It's just terrible.”
She hung the towel on the door handle.
“We didn't tell him anything, you know.
I think it's better that way.
At least he has hope.
So, please be careful, he is so vulnerable now.
It just breaks my heart to see him like this.
It's like he is becoming more transparent every day, and I rub my eyes and squint but still cannot help that feeling.
It upsets him when I stare, so I try to leave him alone a little . . .”
Dalia turned away and went back to the kitchen.
“I'll bring you a glass of water.
It's so hot today.”
She was crying.
Please be careful ...
Yoram sat on his unmade bed watching “Jamie Oliver's Happy Days.”
A bowl of fruit on the dresser was full and almost untouched—that is, if you ignored the flies for which the feast was as pleasant as it was undisturbed.
They hovered around the peaches and slowly rotting pears, landed, and took flight again.
This magnificent spectacle was accompanied by such buzzing that it almost drowned out the cooking show on the small and ancient Sony.
I remember thinking that this fly buzz was the first lively sound I had heard in the house that morning, and for many mornings afterward it remained the only vivid sound of life.
Strangely, life came back to the house after Yoram had left it, but maybe it had been hiding there all the time
and I just couldn't see it.
I was totally baffled by Dalia's request.
Not to tell him?
It's a home hospice we're talking about here, for God's sake, not to mention that it's my first time.
He should have been informed, and certainly not by me.
What about all my rehearsals?
The never-look-down and the steady-voice rules, the simple but sincere truths on death being an integral and inevitable part of life for us all—it's only a matter of time.
The plans I had for him: to watch all of Woody Allen's movies before he dies, to peacefully say goodbye to his sons, his wife, his friends.
He has at least 3 months; it's a lot of time, really.
Maybe choose a good place to be buried in the kibbutz cemetery, to do things he was dreaming of and had never accomplished, go to great restaurants, maybe go to see Jerusalem for the last time . . .
I don't know.
We've never met, but he obviously has dreams, so why not tell him?
Why deny him all of the last things he could do?
And what about the will?
It's a bit cynical, of course, but let's be realistic—this is life.
He has three sons, after all.
Besides, how can he be so blind?
He knows he has cancer, he's lost 30 kilograms, they stopped treatment— what else does a person need to figure it out?
What about his oncologists?
Don't they have some sort of code?
If I were in his place, I would like to know.
I definitely, undoubtedly would.
I would certainly tell her.
I was only 6; fortunately, she died in her sleep.
Actually, I did.
Not me, of course.
Dr. Yakobson did.
Dad died in the hospital alone, he didn't even say goodbye to me and Lior.
They merely told us he had passed away.
They said he had been very sad and didn't want to see anyone.
It must have been depression, but now we have drugs for that.
So, a person can die happier nowadays?
Maybe that's not so absurd.
I heaved a sigh.
All this time, I had been standing two steps behind Yoram, but he was completely preoccupied with the TV show, or maybe the buzz was too loud for him to hear me breathing.
Would I tell him?
It's such a dreadful thought.
His jocular eyes appeared before me.
Would you want me to tell you?
Would you trade the loneliness and despair for the last days of unawareness, fulfillment of dreams, the most intense love I could wrap you in?
His eyes faded, leaving tears in mine.
It's unfair; it is f***ing unfair!
I whispered the last two words, the buzz stopped, and Yoram turned to me smiling.
“Good morning, Doctor.
It's a wonderful morning isn't it?
So kind of you to come.
I feel much better today, you know.
Yesterday, some nice girl in the hospital told me I could really improve in the next couple of months.
What do you think?”
Roman had been in the hospital for 7 days, and no one had visited him.
He had been diagnosed with metastatic lung cancer after losing pounds from an already thin frame.
He had not been very active since retirement, but the cancer left him even more sedentary, and he had to move into a nursing home.
He was admitted to us on this occasion with pneumonia.
Because the infection was located in his upper lung, we uncovered a distant history of tuberculosis exposure and moved him to a negativepressure isolation room.
Each morning, wearing suffocating baby-blue isolation masks, we entered one door and knocked on the second to find our patient hidden inside, wooden and silent like a Russian doll.
He did not have many hobbies and was not much of a reader.
Most mornings we would find him lying in bed and staring at the ceiling, wearing thick glasses that seemed to weigh more than he did and left moist, red welts on the bridge of his nose.
Roman was 68 years old.
He had worked as a carpenter, then as a welder, and finally as a salesman.
He had served in the Army but did not talk much about his experience.
His wife had died 10 years ago, and he had been living alone until the cancer left him too weak to manage on his own.
Years ago, he had been told that he had diabetes and made a point of staying away from cookies and potatoes.
He also smoked heavily and was never able to kick the habit.
He realized he had only months left, and his voice was steady when saying he was not ready to give up.
He wanted to live as long as possible and wanted “everything done.”
His hospital room was like a waiting room— clean, sterile, and value-neutral to the point of blandness.
It was meticulously devoid of any of the signs that accompany patients with histories.
There were no red-eyed pictures of sons, aunts, adoptive grandparents, hard-smiling mothers, or half-lidded uncles.
There were no oddly shaped, dark wood trinkets from the Caribbean.
There were no bowls or plates, no collection of glass figurines or mugs.
Each morning, we stuffed ourselves into our masks and then into this borrowed room and asked what we could do to make him more comfortable.
Perhaps a magazine, a chaplain, or a chat.
He was very pleasant but not ingratiating— calm, almost aloof.
He was polite but didn't have much to say.
We asked how he was feeling.
His cough was improving.
He had no fevers the night before.
He admitted to craving a cigarette.
But he made no mention of a friend or an old neighbor.
There was no personal connection anchoring him to the world.
The stillness of that space was uncomfortable, and we sought to ease this discomfort by swaddling it with a diagnosis.
Maybe he was depressed?
We asked in several different ways, but no, he “was not depressed,” he said.
He just had a lot to think about.
He had a topographic face.
The deep crevasses of his cheeks highlighted a mountain of a nose.
Parallel streams ran across a chronically furrowed brow.
I could picture the smoke curl up from his cigarette, find purchase on his face, and work its way into a crevice.
I can still picture this because I still think about his loneliness.
He was in his final months of life, and there was no one we could call to break the news to.
No one he had known longer than a few days to hold his hand, no one to help him make decisions, and no one to help keep his financial affairs in order.
Usually after a day or two, props from our patients' lives creep in and change the set.
But in Roman's room, there were no block-lettered cards from grandchildren and no photo collages.
There was no evidence of fried chicken contraband from an evening family visit.
There were no half-finished crosswords or dog-eared cowboy novels to suggest he had spent any time outside his own thoughts.
His face seemed to say that a lifetime of circumstance and choices had led to this isolation.
He was coming to face his own regrets within our whitewashed hospital walls.
The hospital is the sieve that catches many of us at our most delicate and trying moments.
Diplomats, teachers, presidents, paupers, and inmates all lay side by side on this grate, and every one of them has a doctor.
We get exposed to a multitude of emotions and experiences, and with many of these—anger, fright, gratitude—I have an improvisational rapport.
Roman's loneliness, though, confounded and devastated me.
I have cared for several patients over the years whose palpable loneliness still shocks me.
And this is not loneliness that 5 minutes of handholding or a visit from the volunteer dog can fix.
This is institutionalized loneliness, refractory loneliness, the end stage of a life that has somehow not gone as planned.
It is mixed with the memory of poor choices and their residual regret.
I am sometimes witness to tragedy that I cannot define, diagnose, manage, or cure.
I am increasingly aware of the tragedy of social isolation, but I have a hard time understanding it.
The mechanism of disease—if one can call it that—is too overwhelming, too multifaceted.

Social isolation is not a medical illness in the way that I was taught to think about illness, but it is often the ubiquitous fact that envelops every medical decision and even haunts the way I think about a patient's terminal illness.
I have never asked a patient why he or she and society had parted ways, but in quiet moments, I occasionally think about it.
Sometimes clues present themselves: an offhand remark about an accident, a history of incarceration or drug abuse, or a financial dispute with a family member.
Roman, however, did not offer many clues.
The next-ofkin information in his chart was blank.
He had a daughter, but they had not spoken in years.
I don't know why.
Sometimes it's better not to know—sometimes too much history can corrupt empathy.
Roman would die soon, and no one who knew his thoughts or his history would be there to witness it.
Even if he had made mistakes, a death not mourned is a tragedy in our rich society.
Maybe society had failed him.
We did not rush in to mortar the gaps.
I realize that in thinking about him, we have drifted far from our medical mooring.
I could stage his lung cancer and offer him a nicotine patch, but these are not the real problems that have washed up on our beachhead.
The hospital is the net that catches societal tragedy all the time, but I struggle with the realization that medicine does not have all the answers.
One morning, I again put on the isolation mask and prepared to knock on Roman's door.
Moving to the left, I caught sight of him sitting on the edge of the bed, a figure framed by the door's rectangular window.
It was a perverse Vermeer.
Light streaming through the window, pushing through the dark interior, illuminating his face and his knobby hands set carefully upon his lap.
His face was upturned to the sky, and I imagined he was looking for answers, searching for closure, trying to come to terms with the way life had turned out.
He sat patiently and was waiting.
He was waiting for a philosopher, a prophet, a king.
He nervously anticipated his historian, his ghosts, his usher.
He was not waiting for his doctor.
One of my first patients as a medical intern was an avowed racist.
Chester (pseudonym) was a lifelong smoker and fan of Southern cuisine whose bad habits finally caught up with him.
His body failing, he turned to our hospital for help only to find me, a black man, as one of the doctors entrusted to extend his life.
The year was 2003, but for a time, it felt more like 1963.
My resident received the call from the emergency department about our new admission just as we were sitting down for dinner.
With the call went my appetite.

I was struggling with the adjustment to internship like everyone else, perhaps more so given my recent 3-year hiatus to study law, so the last thing I needed was something to make my life even more difficult.
But that is exactly what Chester and his family had in store for me.
On our way to Chester's bed in the emergency department, a nurse stepped in front of my path to warn me.
“Watch out for that one,” she said, through a thick Caribbean accent.
I expected her to say he was severely agitated or confused.
Instead, she leaned toward me and lowered her voice.
“He asked a white nurse why there are 'so many niggers' working here, and said that he did not want any 'niggers' taking care of him.”
I asked, in disbelief.
I glanced at my colleagues: a white male medical student and a Jewish woman who was our supervising resident.
It took a few seconds for the resident to regain her composure.
“Don't worry,” she said.
“You don't have to go in.”
“I'll be fine,” I said, as I took a deep breath, “but maybe you should do the talking.”
Chester looked the part of a hospital patient.
A scraggly beard covered much of his ashen face.
His abdomen protruded as if carrying a full-term pregnancy.
His wrinkled hands trembled while adjusting the rubber tubing that fed him oxygen.
He smelled as if he had gone days, maybe weeks, without a bath.
After introductions, Chester grimaced as he strained to lift his head off the pillow.
His voice was feeble and raspy.
“Where's my real doctor?” he asked.
It's common for patients to question the competence of interns and residents and to ask for the attending doctor.
But with what the nurse had told me, his words took on a sinister tone.
Although the resident tried to explain that we were his real doctors, Chester looked past her, scowling at me before his eyes rested on the medical student.
“I only wanna deal with you.”
Our student looked at us with a deer-in-headlights gaze.
The resident clenched her hand, snapping the hook on her ink pen in half.
“I'm in charge, so either you answer my questions, or we find another doctor, which could take hours.”
Chester looked around as he weighed his options.
His physical distress was more important than his mental prejudices.
“Damn you,” he said.
The resident and I gritted our teeth as we worked in tandem and took the usual medical history and performed a physical examination.
Given all the data, pneumonia was the most likely diagnosis.
We wrote admission orders and moved on to other work during the overnight shift.
As I plodded through each successive patient and hour without sleep, I could not get Chester out of my mind.
Prejudice toward black doctors is nothing new, but it was one thing to read about it or hear others recount their experiences and quite another to feel it for myself.
Chester was the first person we saw on morning rounds the next day.
We took a collective deep breath as we entered his room: “Good morning,” the resident said, half-heartedly.
Chester shook his head.
He was unhappy to discover that we were still his doctors.
A middle-aged woman and young man sat at his bedside.
The woman gruffly introduced herself as his oldest daughter.
She wore a T-shirt that proudly displayed the Confederate flag across sagging breasts.
Her son wore a crew cut and had thin forearms covered with tattoos.
His shirt had a smaller Confederate flag on its pocket.
Together, they looked the part of people who might traffic in N-words.
As we left the room, the resident suggested trading Chester to our sister team, where both doctors were white men.
Such a change would have required going up the hospital chain of command.
No matter if she was the one making the request; given the racial element, it would seem that I was the reason behind it.
As the lowest person on the medical hierarchy, this was not the way I wanted to start my career.
So, I convinced her that everything would be fine if we stuck to the medical facts.
In the beginning, that is what we did.
Each day, I updated the family on his progress.
Gradually, however, in the process of tending to his physical ills, I learned tidbits about his life.
He was married for almost 50 years and took care of his wife in her final months after a crippling stroke.
They had 3 daughters and 7 grandkids.
He worked in a factory most of his life.
He loved fishing and baseball.
About 2 weeks into his stay, he finally acknowledged me as a doctor when I asked him one morning how he felt.
I'm gettin' better.”
His daughter softened, too, as she asked about my personal life.
“You got a family?”
“I'm engaged,” I said.
“You'll have to stop workin' so hard if you ever wanna settle down.”
Tension oozed from my back and shoulders.
For the first time, I felt relaxed around them.
It was as if I was having a regular conversation with regular people—not ones who wanted to see me locked up in prison or strung from a tree.
Although I made inroads with earning their trust, our medical efforts ultimately proved futile.
We discovered that his pneumonia stemmed from aggressive lung cancer that had metastasized to his liver.
He also developed a multiresistant infection and acute renal failure.
The verdict was in: Chester was dying, and dying soon.
After some early dissension, his family agreed to sign a DNR order.
The moment came just days later.
Shortly after I arrived for my weekend shift, I received a page from a nursing station.
I returned the call.
“Your patient has died,” a nurse said without emotion.
When I approached Chester's bedside, the room was filled.
A pack of cloudy eyes honed in on me.
I recognized his daughters and some of his grandkids; the rest were new.
I introduced myself to those I had not met as I made my way to Chester.
His eyes were closed.
A blanket covered him from the shoulders down.
He had not been dead long enough to look different than he had in life.
My medical job was easy enough, as I did the formal routine of declaring him dead.
I looked up at his family, scanning from one to the next.
“I'm sorry for your loss.”
After I let that linger for a few seconds, I asked, “Does anyone have questions?”
I had grown so accustomed to asking this to family members of the living that it slipped from my mouth without a thought.
What question could they have other than, “Why couldn't you save him?”
Suddenly on the defensive, I braced for racially tinged criticism and maybe even threats of a lawsuit.
But what came from their mouths was the opposite of hate.
“Thanks for all you did for my daddy,” his eldest daughter said.
“We appreciate all the time you spent with him,” another daughter said.
I nodded, unable to produce an intelligible response.
It was all I could do to leave the room without stumbling against a wall.
Their kind words were the way families reacted when they felt their dead loved ones had been treated with complete dignity.
It was a response that I did not expect.
Despite the flashes of humanity he showed toward the end, I regarded Chester and his family in a mostly negative light.
Like many blacks do, I countered their prejudice with my own: To my eyes, they were high-school dropout, Dixie-flag waving, trailer-park residents; people I saw as fundamentally beneath me.
My family and community had raised me to feel this way, and without much thought, I had obliged.
Yet, here was Chester's progeny, heirs to his bigoted ways, offering nothing but genuine gratitude.
Suddenly, I felt like the racist fool.
Three decades of indoctrination had come under assault.
No longer would I be able to so easily cast people like them in such a monolithic fashion.
Given how things started between us, it seemed our interactions had changed their perspective, too.
Although I might never come over for dinner or marry their daughter, I had played a vital role in something far more crucial—life and death— and they had been pleased with what I had to offer.
In the end, at least for a moment in time, a polarizing black and white landscape had turned a more neutral and friendly gray.
Nowadays, whenever I feel myself on the receiving end of a prejudiced patient or becoming biased against someone based on their appearance or background, I think back to Chester and his family.
Our encounter showed that both sides are capable of something better.
I t was a rather difficult faculty meeting.

The entire time was given to the director of the faculty practice plan, who went over our tenuous financial situation, emphasizing that revenues were down and that we were not likely to get a raise or a bonus at the end of the year.

In discussing the possible remedies, he suggested that we consider reducing some of the frills that we currently enjoyed, the number of fellows that the department was paying, or the educational allowance provided each faculty member.
Finally, he warned us that unless we turned around the downward trend of the department income, we might need to make some cuts in compensation.
I couldn't help but think back to how all of this “prosperity” started.
When I was in medical school during the early 1960s, there was no talk about health care costs, length of stay, or diagnosis-related groups.
We lived in blissful ignorance of the entire issue of who was paying for the care of our patients.
We were exposed only to the charity patients on the ward service and had next to nothing to do with patients on the private service.
In fact, the only thing I knew about the private service was what my advisor told me— do not apply for a private service internship because “the attending will not let you do anything.”
With this advice in mind, I started my medical internship in a hospital that had mostly nonprivate patients.
During our orientation, not a single word was devoted to the cost of medical care.
In fact, the only time any discussion involved money was when we (the medical interns) discovered that the hospital administration trainees were paid four times as much as we were.
We then sent a deputation to meet with the hospital director about this salary discrepancy.
The response was that the hospital had to pay the administration trainees more because they were “college graduates.”
All admitted patients were initially seen by a medical student before the housestaff to ensure that the students would develop adequate history-taking skills.
Except in life-threatening emergencies, these rules were inviolate.
When the chairman himself was admitted for some diagnostic tests, he insisted that the medical student see him first.
He then severely berated the student for not performing a rectal examination.
Attending rounds were of variable length and quality.

We had several truly great attendings who challenged us every day to think in terms of pathophysiology and spent time with the students, honing their skills in summarizing and presenting their findings.
The students were an integral part of rounds and were expected to read about all of the patients and help with the scut work.
In January 1966, everything suddenly changed.
We were never warned about the forthcoming upheaval.
We had never heard the term “Medicaid.”
The only visible change in our daily life was that I suddenly had many more forms to fill out on recently discharged patients.
Sometime during the latter part of February, a check for $1800 made out to me showed up in my mailbox.
It obviously was not mine, because my annual stipend was $3150.
The check was from an insurance company that was the local carrier for Medicaid.
I took the check to the medicine office and gave it to the senior secretary.
As soon as she saw its distinctive color, she immediately knew what it was.
Her only comment was, “Oh, it's one of those!”
She then opened a drawer and pulled out a large stack of similar checks, held together by a wide rubber band.
Although I received a few more similar checks, I stopped thinking about them because someone in the medicine office clearly was in charge of the monies.
This blissful state of ignorance was suddenly brought to a halt several weeks later during our weekly department meeting.
The meeting included the housestaff as well as the faculty and dealt with patient care and medical education issues.
Unlike all previous meetings, the chairman immediately departed from the usual agenda.
He brought with him the large stack of checks.
He put these on the lectern, saying that the current situation was unprecedented.
He asked the audience for their advice on how to dispose of the money.
He thought that these funds should be distributed to the housestaff because, in fact, they took care of the patients.
As soon as he finished that sentence, pandemonium broke out—multiple faculty members started to speak at once, and we (the housestaff) sat dumbfounded.
The meeting rapidly spun out of control and ended with no resolution relating to the distribution of the newly acquired largesse.
The next week, a group of dark-suited men with expensive briefcases showed up to work out the details of the embryonic faculty practice plan.
The housestaff were never again invited to attend faculty meetings that were now devoted exclusively to discussions about money.
At first, not much changed in the daily routine of the hospital, but soon we noticed that several young physicians came to visit and gave grand rounds.
By the next year, a few of these visitors joined the faculty in the various subspecialties of internal medicine.
After my chief residency, I joined the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and eventually returned to the faculty 2 years later as a newly minted junior faculty member.
The most notable change was the presence of many new faculty members in various subspecialties of internal medicine, more than doubling the size of the previously small faculty.
This welcome change came about because of the sudden availability of new funds in the practice plan.
In the 45-plus years after my resident days, the financial aspects of the entire health care system continued to evolve and the changes have been profound and lasting.

During these years, we have learned to live with diagnosisrelated groups, direct and indirect medical education payments to the hospitals by Medicare, extensive and punitive “fraud and abuse” investigations of university medical centers by Medicare, markedly increased demands for documentation, and the addition of compliance officers to the practice plan to avoid being accused of “fraud and abuse” due to “upcoding.”

These changes were followed by the work-hour limitations for house officers and the implementation of the electronic medical record, where a single keystroke can generate an entire page of standardized (and useless) history designed to milk the maximum reimbursement from the “payer sources,” and where “cut and paste” has led to hundreds of pages of identical, extensive, and uninformative notes from every person in contact with the patient.

Although the electronic medical record has markedly improved the quality and efficiency of medical care, it is important to remember that the impetus to develop it was primarily to facilitate billing.
Along with all of these changes, caring for the patient remained the one constant.
The development of evidencebased medicine rationalized therapeutic decisions.
Ready access to electronic references is now at one's fingertips, allowing all of us to get and use the best and most recent information available.
The development of order sets has assured a degree of consistency in the level of care, but at the cost of eliminating the need for reflection and critical thinking by the house officer.

The relentless pressure for reduced length of stay has created an avalanche of paperwork that has to be completed by the house officer, all too frequently during the hours set aside for teaching rounds, limiting time for discussion of potentially important considerations for patient care.
Finally, the typing of extensive and redundant daily notes has replaced the narrative text, which was an important stimulus of critical thinking.

During the past 45 years of making attending rounds, I learned all of the new fads, mastered the use of the computer, became familiar with the lingo of modern statistics, and can even define and calculate “prior probability.”
It is just not as much fun...
Standing at 6 feet, 4 inches tall, I am easily spotted by my friends and colleagues in the daily rush of people moving through the hallways at the hospital.
When you add my thin build and comically short white coat to the picture, I never exactly blend into the crowd.
One day, near the beginning of my third year of medical school, I was standing in the lobby waiting for an elevator when a boy walked by, holding his mom's hand.
He suddenly stopped dead in his tracks, pointed up at me, and said: “Look, Mommy!
“I've told you it's rude to stare!”
the mom said, quickly pulling her child away.
I laughed awkwardly as I stepped onto the elevator.
After all, hardly a week had gone by since I had begun clinical rotations that a patient or family member had not commented on my height as I walked into their room.
It had become a source of pride, a part of my daily routine.
It was normal.
Four months later, I found myself in that same elevator, grimacing and trying to hide my pain from the other passengers.
For the past month, I had been waking in the early morning hours with terrible discomfort in my right leg.
The deep ache would keep me awake for an hour or so before the fatigue from the long hours of surgery and deliveries would catch up with me and pull me back into a deep sleep.
That morning, though, the soreness had not faded.
By the time I left the labor and delivery ward at the end of my shift, my leg and abdomen were on fire with a pain that grew worse with every movement.
As I stepped off the elevator and walked through the doors of the emergency department, thoughts of “med student syndrome” ran through my head.
This is probably nothing but gas and my imagination running wild.

As the resident and attending physician finished their examinations, I was convinced that I should have spent the evening studying (or at least watching TV) rather than humiliating myself in front of future colleagues.
A couple of hours later, the emergency medicine attending physician walked into my room and slid the glass door closed behind him.

His friendly, optimistic countenance from earlier that evening was gone, replaced by another: the all-too-familiar expression that I—even in my short career—recognized as the look of a doctor who was about to deliver bad news.
“I just read your CT scan.
Your appendix looks normal, but there's a large mass in your pelvis.
The official read from radiology isn't back yet, but it looks suspicious for a hematoma versus a benign tumor versus some type of sarcoma...”
I remember very little of what he said after that.
For the third time in this story, I found myself in that exact same elevator.
As I sat silently in my wheelchair on the way to my hospital room, I looked up at the nurses and doctors about to begin their daily routine—my daily routine.
In that moment, I would have given anything for my life to just go back to normal.
The next week was a blur of scans, biopsies, and consultations from seemingly every service in the hospital.
I will never forget the expressions on my parents' faces as the doctor delivered the biopsy results.
My father—a man who always had an answer for every question and a solution to any problem—looked absolutely helpless as he asked, “Ewing's sarcoma?
Scott, what does that mean?”
I sat there silently, unable to answer him.
This couldn't be happening to me.
I'm supposed to be the one treating people and going home at the end of the day.
Despite all the love I had surrounding me from family and friends, I felt utterly alone.
The people who truly cared about me and the people who were caring for me seemed to be two separate groups.
I never had trouble feeling considerable sympathy for my patients, but genuine empathy?
Could these doctors possibly know how my parents used to tell me bedtime stories about foxes or how I used to dream about being a fighter pilot when I grew up?
Why would they need to know that?
They were going to provide medical care and nothing more.
For me, that had been normal.
Just as I was preparing to leave the hospital, I heard a knock at the door, and a rather short woman in a long white coat entered the room.
She introduced herself as the hematology–oncology fellow who would be taking care of me over the next several months.
I was impressed by both her kindness and her knowledge as we talked about my immediate prognosis and long-term expectations.
As she sat at the foot of my bed listening to me talk about how eager I was to get back to school, however, something changed.
To this day I don't know if it was just my imagination, but I could have sworn that I saw her eyes start to tear up.
Here was someone who, despite meeting me only 15 minutes ago, seemed to care about what happened to me.
Just for an instant, all of the loneliness and turmoil of the previous week melted away and were replaced by a new feeling—peace.
Maybe the group of people who were caring for me did not have to be separate from those who cared about me after all.
For the next 10 months, I was wheeled through the very same hallways in which I had once towered over others.
I would often look up at the doctors (and even medical students) who, despite working with me only months before, no longer even recognized me.
I would have given anything to go back to how life had been before my diagnosis.
At the same time, though, I began to understand both the joys and frustrations my patients had been experiencing each day.

I even witnessed how the terror of contracting an infection without a working immune system could be driven away by a nursing assistant with a welltimed orange Popsicle delivery in the middle of the night.
Finally, radiation and 17 cycles of chemotherapy had done their job, and it was time to return to school.
During my first week back, I found myself standing in the same elevator, once again wearing my comically short white coat.
A woman walked onto the elevator carrying her young daughter, who looked over at me with an amazed expression on her face.
“Yes, he's very tall isn't he, sweetie?”
Her mom observed with a laugh.
I couldn't help grinning as I stepped out of the elevator and into the child psychiatry unit where I was just beginning my rotation.
Just as I had hoped and prayed for since the early days of my illness, things were finally back to normal.
That day, a 16-year-old girl arrived on our floor after a suicide attempt.
I spent that afternoon speaking with her and her family, trying to understand the events that had nearly led to tragedy.
At the end of the day, I headed back to my apartment with a heavy heart.
I kept thinking about the expression of helplessness on her mother's face, and how it reminded me of my own mother only a year ago.
As I tried to fall asleep that night, I could picture her mom sitting in an empty house, just wishing that she could have her daughter safely home with her.
As I encountered more patients on the wards, I kept coming home worried about them and imagining myself in their positions.
Though I was doing well in my rotations and was relating to patients in an exciting and meaningful new way, I found that doing my job had actually become more difficult than ever before.
It wasn't until midsummer when I finally managed to visit my friend, a family physician in a southeastern city in Iran near the Pakistan border.
We had worked jointly on a few medical research projects, and at the time he was fulfilling a public service obligation, as was his wife in a nearby town.
I arrived, drained, at his office as dusk fell.

His setup was just as I had imagined: a weather-beaten building, rudimentary equipment, a very basic medication cabinet, a few rooms in which to see patients, a terraced residence in the rear, and a dilapidated Land Rover next to the beat-up ambulance.
My friend read my thoughts from the look on my face.
“You get used to it,” he said.
“I'm supposed to leave early in the morning,” I murmured.
We sat in his office to catch up just as the call came from his wife.
Doubled over from an acute attack of abdominal pain, she was on the way to the local hospital.
She needed him.
Throwing on his coat, my friend asked, “Cover me for a few hours.
I'll be back soon.”
Again he read my face.
It's just you.
But nothing happens at night.
Anyway, you have my number,” he shouted over his shoulder.
Here I was, suddenly alone, with the silent, bitter-cold night of the desert, the chirping of crickets, the distant barking dogs.
I turned on the radio for company and started working at my laptop.
It was past midnight when I heard a knock at the door.
I was apprehensive and did not answer at first.
The knocking was repeated again and again.
“Who is it?”
I asked, in Persian.
The answer came in English.
“Please open the door.
We need help.
I opened the door to a Pakistani family.
A worn, withered man embraced a small girl of about 5.
A woman, utterly exhausted, held something wrapped in her arms.
I assumed it was an infant.
I caught their anxiety, their panic.
I reflexively let them in.
The mother unwrapped the infant and a stench filled the air.
The little girl started crying.
I saw a half-naked, dehydrated infant lying immobile in diarrhea.
I thought he might be dead.
I moved closer and heard his gasping breath.
Wash your baby under the tap.”
