I ran to find a catheter and an IV setup.
I opened the window and the door to let in fresh air, placed the setup and the catheter by the infant, and gave his mother a towel to dry him.
I searched the cabinet to find IV fluids and when I turned back, I saw that the mother had inserted the catheter and started the IV.
Her husband explained.
“She works in a clinic in Pakistan and knows some procedures.”
Thank goodness, I thought to myself.
I had been worrying about getting venous access in this infant.
I connected the IV fluids and checked his vital signs.
“Where have you come from?”
“We are from Pakistan.
We were displaced because of the flood,” the man replied.
“But that flood is far to the East, very distant from here . . .
.How—” The man interrupted.
“I am a school teacher.
The flood washed everything away.
We lost everything.
We had no other choice but to come to a friend of mine who lives in Iran.
We came 1000 km in 6 days and were kept at the border for 2 days.
They would not let us in.
We had no papers.
Our situation got worse.
My son got sick, and no medical care was available.”
I searched my pack and found chocolate and biscuits.
They were ravenous and thirsty.
I gave the small girl the chocolate and the parents the biscuits.
The man continued.
“I found an Afghani near the border who told me he would take us into Iran for no charge.
We followed him, all of us—my wife, my daughters, and my son.”
I interrupted him.
At this, the man began to weep.
The mother turned her face to conceal her tears, and the little girl hugged her father frantically.
The man spoke through his tears.
“In the mountains the Afghani and his friend demanded money; we had agreed there would be no charge.
I told them that we had no money.
They threatened to leave us alone in the mountains to die unless I gave them my daughter.
He said he wanted a wife.
I told him that she was only a child, but he insisted that she was ready for marriage.”
My mouth hung open in horror.
“That seemed the only way to save us.
They took us near this city and went into the mountains with our daughter...”
I was in shock.
Having 2 daughters of my own, I could empathize deeply with this man.
I raised my head and managed to say, “That was the best decision in that situation— your only option.
I know that police will help you get your daughter back.”
The man quickly replied.
“Please do not call the police.
They will only send us back, and we will not get our daughter back.”
“You need to get some rest.
Let me find something for you.”
I went to the next room and called my friend, speaking in Persian.
I subconsciously noted the father's apprehension, that I might be calling the police or the officials, whereas I was merely asking for some blankets and the way to get into the residence.
When I returned to the office, no one was there.
They were gone.
I searched around the clinic but found no sign of them.
The man had not known that I was just a visiting friend, that I had been calling this friend to whom I spoke Persian.
Protecting his family, the man assumed I was speaking with the authorities, and he fled with them.
I looked for them.
I searched throughout the city the next day, as you would have.
I never found them.
I had lost them.
I doubted that I would ever experience the loss of a patient such as this again.
“He is 69 years old, on an experimental treatment for metastatic colon cancer, and he has free air,” explained the emergency department physician.
It was a Sunday afternoon, and I was the surgeon on call.
The CT scan had been done without intravenous contrast, and the cava was flat, from which I inferred that his renal function was poor.
He had liver metastases, but there was still plenty of normal liver, and the metastases alone would not explain this much ascites.
There was scattered free air, but too much ascites to be from an acute perforation, and the pleural effusions suggested a more chronic process.
There was so much edema of the soft tissues that the interface between fat, muscle, and bowel had been lost.
This was not a man who had been holding his own against metastatic cancer; this was a man who had been in the process of dying for some time, and then he perforated.
From the scan, I knew that his lab results would show elevated creatinine, anemia, and profound hypoalbuminemia.
I knew what the tissues would look like in the operating room, and I had a pretty good idea what the ICU would look like postoperatively—the ventilator, the drips, the days or weeks ahead.
As I gathered myself and headed to his room, I realized that my job would be to explain to him that we could operate, but we could not heal him.

“Enough is enough; we need to let you go”—this is a tough message to give to a stranger, especially one who has the means and motivation to cycle through all of the conventional treatments for colon cancer and then seek experimental options.
Why in the world should he trust me?, I wondered.
She was a quiet, elegant woman with shoulder-length, silver hair, a gray sweater, long wool skirt, and sensible shoes.
She stood closely by his side, her arms clasping a notebook to her chest.
His mouth agape, his breathing was deep and rhythmic, too regular, and too fast—a sign of sepsis.
I introduced myself, confirmed that she was his wife, and gently shook him awake until his eyes opened.
He focused, though only for a moment.
He didn't respond to my questions, so his wife did the talking.

I took my time, asking about more than I needed to: his symptoms, the onset of pain, past treatments and the experimental protocol, his strength, weight, quality of life these past days and weeks, how many children they had and where they lived, how long they had been married, what work he was doing or did.
I listened to his lungs and his heart, and I looked for a long time at his hands—the fragile skin and the stark crevasses between the carpal bones where the interosseus muscles were supposed to be.
I didn't need to touch his abdomen; it was obvious from the way that he was breathing that he had peritonitis, and I knew from the CT that he had a perforation.
I gathered the information carefully and methodically, because I needed to be certain that I had not made a mistake.
Just as important, I needed them to be confident that I knew who he was, that I knew what had happened, and that I knew what we should do.
I needed them to trust me with what I was about to tell them.
His wife had durable power of attorney, but they had not discussed limits on his care, how far to carry things, what to do when the treatment stopped working, or when the end was in sight.
They hadn't discussed it with their local oncologist—an experienced, wonderful physician who is smart and compulsive and kind, and who I would let care for my own family.
He happened to be out of town that day, or I would have gladly deferred this discussion to him.
They didn't discuss it with their oncologist at the university, whom I know well.
He defies the stereotype of an academic oncologist—an attending who carries a pager, knows his patients personally, and combines the best of academic expertise and human compassion.
They didn't discuss it with their primary care physician, who had cared for him for over a decade, but whom he had seen only rarely since he started cancer treatment.
All of them knew that he would die of his cancer.
None of them had discussed this moment—what happens at the end.
“This is what I believe has happened,” I explained.
“These are the things that can be done.
We can operate, but we would have to leave him on the ventilator.”
Slowly, I shifted from talking about you to talking about him, when his eyes did not open, when he showed no sign that he heard what I was saying.
I was taking care of her now, as much as I was taking care of him.
I was touched by her bravery.
“Under the best of circumstances, I wouldn't expect to be able to take him off the ventilator for days or weeks; I doubt that he would leave the ICU.
I would not expect him to survive to leave the hospital; he is not likely ever to be well enough to get more treatment for his cancer.
Do you know what he would want?”
She had not asked a single question, and her expression gave me no hint of what she was feeling.
I had tried to be gentle, but I felt cruel and blunt and ashamed of myself, as I bludgeoned her with this explanation.
I was a stranger to this woman.
What gave me the right to say these things?
“Can I take him home?”
“I want to take him home.”
Just like that.
It took one call to hospice.
Three hours later, he was home.
He had pain medication.
Their children arrived.
That afternoon, he died peacefully in the bed that he had shared with his wife for more than 40 years.
It took me a couple of hours that Sunday to do my work.
It would have taken much longer to have prepared him for the operating room and completed his surgery.
From the operating room he would have gone to the ICU, where we would have kept him alive on a ventilator for quite a long time, because we are very good at supporting blood pressure and ventilation.
A week later he might have been a little better but still on the ventilator, with a tube in his trachea and one in his stomach, another in his bladder, a central line or two, perhaps a rectal tube.
He would be swollen, his fragile skin would blister, and he would not know where he was.
At least he would not be in pain.
When patients survive ICU stays like this, they don't tell me that they remember the pain much.
At the end of the week, we would be discussing withdrawal of support, but it's unlikely that we would be able to have a meaningful discussion with him.
His care would cost tens of thousands of dollars in the first couple of days; in a month, hundreds of thousands.
His cancer would go untreated.
I should not have been called to see this patient.

Decisions like the one we had to make that day should have been made among friends and family or in the company of his family physician or oncologist, at a time when he was awake and at his best, when he was not in pain, and he could remember who he was and he could explain to those he would leave behind how he wanted to be remembered.
To have to make such decisions the way we did that day— counseled by a stranger in the sterile alcove of a busy emergency room—is not what any of us would want.
That it turned out the way it did, I believe, was fortunate.

It would have been so easy for the powerful momentum of modern medicine to have carried his broken body into the operating room and from there to the ICU, where he would be nurtured by the finest medical technology and the clinical compassion of strangers.
Recently, we have been warned that government “death panels” would knock us off.

The provision in the new health care legislation, which said that private, end-oflife discussions between a patient and his or her physician would be reimbursable every 5 years, somehow became a sinister governmental strategy to kill us quickly and save resources.
It disappeared from the President's health care legislation, was quietly added back as a Medicare provision, but disappeared again when the new Medicare guidelines came out.
Can this sensible, thoughtful proposal really be so objectionable?
“I want to take him home.”
I am so grateful to this man's brave wife, who knew exactly what her husband would have wanted.
She didn't need me to tell her what kind of man her husband was, to discuss with her the meaning of life or the nuances of medical futility.
What she needed was someone to help her see what was about to happen in the world of medicine—a world that was foreign to her but one in which I travel every day.
These conversations are difficult for me.
They are so much harder than explaining the rationale for an operation, the side effects, or the risks; I don't feel that I am very good at them.
But every one of my patients is going to die one day.
Like it or not, I should have these conversations earlier, more often, and more comfortably.
If that makes me part of a death panel, well, I suppose I can live with that.
As a primary care physician, sometimes the smallest requests can create the biggest challenges.
Between patients, I peeked at my electronic inbox.

Among the usual prescription refills, there was a request for a callback from Mrs. Talbot, a spry elderly patient of mine with well-controlled high blood pressure, osteoarthritis, and a remote history of cancer.
She was an active participant at the local senior center, had lunch regularly with friends on her fixed income, and loved to talk about her grandchildren.
Speaking with her would probably brighten my day.
“Every year, I receive a subsidy from the state to help cover the costs of my apartment,” she told me when I reached her.
“Yesterday around the usual deadline, I went to the office, and they told me that I was too late, that the deadline had been last week.”
“That's terrible, Mrs. Talbot,” I replied.
“But why are you calling me?”
“They told me that if I got a note from my doctor, they could accept my application late.”
“Did they want information about your medical conditions?
Did they want to know if you were sick?”
She was actually one of my healthiest patients.
“No,” Mrs. Talbot said.
“They told me that the note just needs to say that I am under the care of a doctor.”
She wasn't asking me to lie and say that she was sick, but the state office had put her in a difficult position.
If she could provide a note from a doctor— even if it provided only the most rudimentary information—she would be able to imply that there was a medical reason for her tardiness.
At this point, I was really angry.
A glitch between my patient and a government bureaucrat created a problem that was now sitting in my lap.
A deadline had been missed, and I was being asked to inappropriately use my medical authority for a quick fix.
It was an abuse of the physician's social power and a waste of my time.
In general, this power has appropriate and necessary relevance.
Sick people are exempt from certain responsibilities that healthy people must uphold.
They can miss work to rest and get well.
If disabled, they cannot work, and the government and society support their needs financially.
Our letters help obtain discounts for disrupted air travel due to illness.
A physician, through his or her clinical assessment, stands at the cusp between deciding whether a person is healthy or too ill to pay.
But these powerful conclusions can sometimes be very difficult to make.

Although I can determine if a patient has real neurologic compromise, how can I really know if the low back pain is severe enough for the patient to stay home, not work, and not contribute to society's pot?
My first encounter with this issue came just after I finished residency.
My nephew called requesting a favor.
A senior in high school, he was planning for “Senior Ditch Day.”
The principal had told the students that if they missed school, they would be suspended with a serious mark on their permanent records— unless they had a doctor's note, of course.

I weighed the issues; I wanted to nurse my image of the “cool” aunt by providing the note for my adolescent nephew, but I realized that doing so undermined the authority of the physician and abused the social power that our profession strives so hard to earn.
When I told him I could not write his note, he understood.
I felt guilty but knew that it was just one of those impossibleto-fulfill requests that I would get used to as I grew as a physician.
As a primary care physician, I frequently get requests from my patients that challenge the proper use of my authority.
They ask for completion of forms for the utility company that asks me to document a medical reason to protect them from having their electricity shut off.
Each request has to be weighed carefully.
There are some cases in which all physicians would agree merit intervention.
On the other hand, some patients really have no medical reason to involve us.
Many cases lie somewhere in between.
At the start of the summer, some patients get behind in payments but still want to use their air conditioning.
When I have an elderly patient with chronic obstructive lung disease, this is a pretty easy decision.
Lack of access to air conditioning during the dog days of summer could lead to a life-threatening pulmonary exacerbation.
Similarly, a patient with type 1 diabetes mellitus who uses certain types of insulin needs access to proper refrigeration to prevent a life-threatening metabolic emergency.
But how do I handle a request from a patient with obstructive sleep apnea who uses a breathing machine to help provide appropriate oxygenation at night?
This patient has probably lived with the condition for a long time before diagnosis without hospital visits for emergencies.
However, when a person has this condition and does not receive appropriate treatment, the risk for hypertension— and therefore heart attacks and strokes—increases.
What is the true definition of “life-threatening”?
Immediate threat of death?
Something that will worsen health over time and may eventually include grave consequences?
Some requests push the boundary of appropriateness.
I once had a request from a patient in his 40s who took pills for hypertension— his only medical problem.
As much as I believed he should have access to electricity, he did not have an appropriate medical justification that would have allowed me to complete the form and maintain my integrity.
I politely explained that this was not the kind of situation that fit the utility company's definition of “life-threatening.”
On the phone with Mrs. Talbot, I tried to keep the aggravation from my voice.
The problem was a miscommunication or change in schedule between Mrs. Talbot and the housing authority.
It had nothing to do with her health and certainly nothing to do with me.
I explained to her cautiously that I thought this request was not an appropriate use of physician authority and that I felt badly that the state put her in such a predicament.
“But if you don't write this letter, I will need to pay $400 more a month!”
This felt so unfair.
In addition to whether this request was an appropriate use of my medical authority, it was also a question of time and money.
There are just so many other more pressing and more important activities that I must do to help take care of my patients.
I also do not get paid for taking the time to write such a letter in my current reimbursement scheme.
Some offices now reasonably charge a fee for completion of such forms.
The additional work requires the physician's appropriate judgment and time.
However, the patients who request this paperwork are often those with the least disposable income.
So we do what we can, realizing that we will just have to stay at work a little later.
Or worry about burnout.
Or refuse to help.
Or wonder if society can find another way to support the impoverished and reduce the burden on the medical system.
As I considered Mrs. Talbot's call, I told her that I would call her back with my decision.
But I felt that I had no choice.
When my nephew asked for a letter for Senior Ditch Day, there was no medical justification worthy of his request, and he could live with the consequences of his actions.
The stakes here felt too high.
I wanted more time to deliberate, but my next patient was waiting for me, my inbox had more refills waiting, and 2 more patients requested return calls.
And I had to get home to make dinner and help my kids with homework.
Just writing the letter would not only help my patient but would be faster and easier.
I didn't have to lie; she was in fact under my care.

I wanted to call the housing authority, verify the situation, tell them that all patients (at least those with appropriate access to our health care system) are “under the care of a physician” and that they were wasting my time.
I would tell them to be sure to help my patient because the bureaucrats played with the due date of her forms.
But after near-daily challenges with insurance companies over medication authorization and other requests for utilities help, I needed to pick my battles carefully.
I have only so much time and energy to advocate for my patients.
This battle would have to wait another day.
I called Mrs. Talbot back and told her the letter would be mailed to her home.
“But what is the most effective way to teach our fellows the ethical conduct of research?”
As if a subliminal alarm clock had just sounded, I awoke with a start.

I was attending a national conference on research ethics, my interest spurred by my dual role at the time as chairman of the hospital institutional review board and director of the pulmonary fellowship training program.
However, when the discussion veered to the moral and regulatory nuances of competence, equipoise, and substituted judgment, mental exhaustion gained the upper hand.

Dozing intermittently on the last conference day, I briefly rallied for this final discussion, yet like many verbal exchanges among intelligent, impassioned experts, the debate yielded more questions than answers.
The meeting adjourned, and I welcomed the transition from the chilly conference room to the warm autumn sunshine as I stepped outside in our nation's capital.
With several hours before my evening train home and nowhere special to go, I wandered across the mall and soon found myself at the entrance to the United States Holocaust Memorial Museum.
In the 10 years since it had opened, I had hoped to visit but not yet had the chance.
Without children in tow or a pressing agenda at hand, I knew the present opportunity would not come again soon.
I stepped inside.
The museum exhibit was arranged chronologically, carefully cataloging the persecution and destruction of Europe's unwanted peoples—Jews, homosexuals, Roma, the mentally infirm.
To walk attentively from room to room was to witness genocide unfold in slow motion; rants transitioning to goosesteps, smashed windows into shattered lives.
Viewing the collection was like watching freeze-frame video of a seismic eruption.
One intuitively understood that a cataclysmic destruction was at hand but was helpless to intervene or refrain from watching.
The most graphic images appeared toward the end of the exhibit.
In one of the final rooms was a photographic display at floor level, surrounded by a 4-foot barrier.
As I approached the low wall, I looked over to see the distorted faces of camp inmates who were the subjects of brutal experimentation.

Concentration camp physicians had exposed prisoners to extremes of water temperature and barometric pressure to determine human tolerance to the elements that might be encountered by pilots forced to ditch their aircraft.
I assumed that the barrier was intended to limit young children from seeing the anguish of the victims.

Silently, I wondered which was the greater concern of the museum personnel: traumatizing children with images of physical torture or traumatizing them with the knowledge that physicians were willing participants in inflicting such torture.
I learned from the museum that law enforcement personnel visited the exhibits on special tours, to understand the potential for abuse that accompanies power.
In recalling the morning's unresolved question, I realized that physician-trainees could benefit from a similar program.
One visit here would probably provide a more lasting lesson on the necessary boundaries of scientific inquiry than a series of classroom lectures on medical ethics.
Of course, it was hardly practical to bring our fellows to Washington, DC, to calibrate their moral compass.
Perhaps there was another way.
A few weeks later, I prepared for our Friday morning conference on chest imaging.
The goal of the conference was to present unknown films with limited clinical information, to teach fellows to form a differential diagnosis based on the radiographic findings alone.
I pulled a specific set of images and headed to the conference room.
As the session started, I placed a posterior anterior and lateral chest radiograph on the viewbox.
“What do you see?”
“A left upper lobe lung nodule,” came the reply.
Clearly, there was a sharply circumscribed opaque shadow—an obvious white oval within the darkened lung field.
“And on the lateral?” I continued.
Despite intense scrutiny, a nodule could not be seen.
“Can we see a CT?”
the fellows asked.
I provided CT images of the chest.
The “lung nodule” was now visible as a calcified pleural plaque, not a nodule at all.
I highlighted the intended lesson: A 2-dimensional image does not always accurately portray 3-dimensional reality.
Sometimes one has to look deeper.
Customarily, we moved rapidly through films in this conference, but instead of showing the next case, I digressed to provide background on the patient of this particular radiograph.
As a 17-year-old teenager, this young man and his father were imprisoned in a Nazi labor camp during World War II.
In the winter of 1944, the teenager developed pleurisy, fever, anorexia, and weight loss.
Unable to work, he was brought to the infirmary.
The camp physician improvised a drainage system for the effusion, offered the meager services available at the time to assist a Jewish inmate, and turned his attention to other matters.
Returned to the barracks and expected to work, the young man stood little chance of surviving the elements.
To even the odds, his father implored a fellow inmate—a dentist when one's profession mattered—to pry the father's gold crowns from his teeth for use as currency.
The dentist complied, using the crude utensils available in camp, and the painfully extracted bribe was offered to the barracks guard.
A few grams of gold netted several weeks of barracksrestriction for the teenage son—enough time to regain the strength needed to endure the rigors of camp life.
Even considering that life in a labor camp had little value, this was a bargain price, readily paid by the father, to ensure his son's survival.
Father and son were liberated the following spring, and the films were obtained years later as part of a preoperative pulmonary assessment.
We digressed further and discussed the actions of the camp doctor and their ethical ramifications.
Did a physician's obligations to a prisoner differ from his responsibilities to a noninmate patient when resources were scarce?
If a physician modified a procedure to provide treatment, was this medical care or experimentation?
At a time of medical privation, was it ethical to render acute treatment but insufficient after-care?
And a final, broader question: How could one ensure that external societal forces would not corrupt a physician's moral judgment?
This last question proved the thorniest to answer.
As the discussion continued, a parallel story occupied my thoughts.
About 500 km to the east, at around the same time, a 14-year-old girl was having a very different encounter with a very different Nazi physician: selection day.

As she gathered the courage to walk past a review platform with her fellow inmates, she rotated her torso laterally so that the inspecting physician would not see the inflamed and excoriated insect bites and sores on her back.
The subtle feint was skillfully executed, and the doctor triaged her to the right—to survival.
The girl's mother had not been so lucky.
With an indifferent wave of the hand that belied the magnitude of the summary judgment, the camp physician dispatched her to the left when she first arrived at Auschwitz.
Her first day at the camp was her last.
In cancer research, much effort has been expended to understand the signals and pathways that turn a cell malignant.
But how does one explain the physician who has become malignant?
Here were two men, steeped in the same medical tradition, subject to the same propaganda, and caught within the same political and military system.
Yet while one retained a measure of human compassion and personal ethics, the other was bereft of both.
The medical profession is not a job; it is not even a calling.
It is a gift we are given, to touch another's life in awesome and profound ways.
We discard this gift at our own peril.
Primum non nocere, our medical forefathers advised.
First, do no harm.
Once we cross this boundary, all is lost.
The radiology conference ended.

I left with a sensation reminiscent of that autumn afternoon in Washington, warmed not by a gentle breeze but from the knowledge that a chest x-ray teaching session could provide something more simply than a lesson in radiology.
Of the questions I had anticipated, one remained unasked: How do you know so much about the background of this patient?
I had rehearsed my answer.
“From stories told to me, when I reached an age of remembrance and understanding,” I envisioned replying, “of my father's pleurisy, my mother's back, and my grandfather's gold teeth.”
a World Away
It was almost lunchtime.
Outside the hospital in Rwanda, patients' family members formed a line behind a large, steaming pot of potatoes, awaiting the distribution of food to bring to their hospitalized loved ones.
The women wore kitenges, patterned cloths wrapped around their waists that gently swept the sun-dried dirt as they walked.
The men wore brightly colored shirts, unbuttoned to cope with the blistering heat.
The single-file queue looked like a psychedelic caterpillar looping around the rural hospital's inpatient ward.
Inside the hospital, the scene was less magical.
I stood in a small, secluded private room with my patient, Egide, a 42-year-old Rwandan farmer.
The rest of the ward was packed, often 2 patients to a bed.
Egide was used to isolation.
Not only was he HIV-positive, but he had a large, strange mass growing from around his anal area.
The mass prevented him from sitting and forced him to walk hunched over, clutching a wooden stick.
Efforts to help patients in the developing world with HIV, tuberculosis, and other infectious diseases are wellknown.
Egide was able to benefit from the hospital's antiretroviral program.
In the past, he had been compliant with his HIV medications.
However, his wife had died, and without her encouragement, it had now been 2 years since he was last seen in the HIV clinic.
He now likely faced the HIV-associated complication of a secondary malignancy, which sent him desperately shuffling back to the hospital.
What could now be done for his cancer?

As a third-year internal medicine resident in Boston, I assumed that the day-to-day logistics of treating cancer in the developing world must differ significantly from those in the United States, but I wondered: Are the larger issues that cancer patients and their physicians face truly that unique?
As an aspiring oncologist, I traveled to Rwanda as a volunteer to find out.
Egide's fly-infested room stunk of feces, and his mass rendered him incontinent of stool.
He had no family campaigning for his care.
He had become the proverbial circus sideshow act, generating curiosity and disgust as to what lay behind the swinging metal door to his private room.
Caring for Egide would surely illustrate the practical barriers to successful cancer care in Rwanda and the differences in how treatment is delivered.

I soon learned, however, how strikingly universal the fundamental issues related to cancer treatment are around the world, even reaching into the confines of Egide's 80-square-foot room in the Rwandan countryside.
The first challenge in treatment, as for any cancer patient, began with a diagnosis.
Most diseases at the Rwandan hospital were diagnosed by physical examination.
Making a cancer diagnosis, however, is more complicated.

The task of acquiring a biopsy of tissue requires years of specialized training, including experience in endoscopy, bronchoscopy, and invasive radiology to properly place the diagnostic needle in the suspected tumor.
For the first time, Egide's anal mass offered him an advantage.
It was immediately available to the biopsy needle.
A local Rwandan doctor performed Egide's biopsy, but who would provide the exacting eye of the pathologist to scrutinize the tissue through a microscope and put a scientific name to the mass?
I discovered that Rwanda had only 5 pathologists, 40 miles away in the capital city of Kigali, who served the entire Rwandan population of approximately 10 million people.
The local Rwandan doctors informed us that it would take months to hear back from the pathologists in Kigali.
Luckily, I was able to arrange for the biopsy to be transported and analyzed in Boston.
We soon learned that Egide had a type of cancer called plasmablastic lymphoma, a rare non-Hodgkin lymphoma associated with HIV.
As this was the first time this particular diagnosis was faced by the local medical staff, who was to determine the plan of care?
The local Rwandan doctors had a breadth of skills: setting broken bones, delivering babies, and diagnosing internal medicine conditions by physical examination alone.
But they lacked expertise in cancer care, a condition rarely diagnosed and even more rarely treated in their patient population.
Omnipresent even in rural East Africa
, the Internet helped provide the solution.
We posted Egide's case on a Web site run by the Global Health Delivery Project (www .ghdonline.org), on which local providers pose questions to international experts.
It was determined that Egide needed chemotherapy consisting of at least 6 treatments of cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone, priced at $80 a treatment.
Despite his poor prognosis, with proper care, Egide might be cured.
In a country of limited resources, many considered the cancer regimen extravagant.

Similar to the ongoing U.S. debates about heroic medical efforts to avoid “pulling the plug on grandma,” our Rwandan hospital team asked: Should limited funding be spent on a patient with a poor prognosis?
Would financial support for expensive chemotherapy be better spent on preventive services that could potentially help more patients?
As an alternative to chemotherapy, we considered a purely palliative care–based approach.
Yet shortly thereafter, a few local doctors informed me that many Rwandans view palliative care as a symbol of a failed medical system—one that cannot cure its patients.
Palliative care was viewed as an approach where nothing more aggressive could be done, not a situation where nothing more aggressive should be done.

The Rwandan doctors all had an anecdote to share about a time they had proposed a palliativecare plan for a patient, only to face an angry family demanding that the patient be transported to more developed medical systems in Uganda or Tanzania.
Their stories reminded me that every day, patients from all across New England travel to Boston for their “one last chance” at survival.
Only after all attempts have failed using aggressive curative treatment are purely palliative approaches finally accepted, and even then with great reluctance.
Egide, not surprisingly, wanted the aggressive approach.
For Egide and the local doctors in charge of his care, pure palliation would confirm that the Rwandan health care system had failed.
For Egide, as for many of my patients in Boston, even a sliver of hope is enough to shut out the reality of one's own mortality.
A few weeks later, with the generous support of an American international health organization, Egide's chemotherapy arrived.
His private room quickly became a hubbub of commotion with doctors, nurses, and even inquisitive patients checking out the scene.
When the chemotherapy started, Egide smiled and clapped ecstatically.
After the last drop of fluorescent red doxorubicin infused into his veins, Egide expressed disappointment to learn that he would have to wait 3 weeks for additional chemotherapy.
Shortly after his treatment began, I returned to Boston to complete my residency.
A few months later, I learned that Egide died.
Despite my attempts to learn what happened, I never got a detailed explanation.
I wondered if the chemotherapy had caused him undue immunosuppression, increasing his susceptibility to a myriad of infections, or if his cancer had progressed, undeterred by the chemotherapy.
I cannot help but wonder whether the heroic attempt made in Egide's private room was a success or failure.
Did my involvement as a transient American internal medicine resident inspire advocacy for the abandoned patient and provide guidance for the needed complex cancer treatment?
Or did sympathy for one patient's plight come at the expense of helping more patients in need?
The details of caring for Egide in the sweltering Rwandan countryside differed starkly from the patients I managed on the oncology floors in the cold Boston winter.

Yet, as a physician caring for a cancer patient, maintaining hope in the face of a life-threatening diagnosis, understanding the hesitancy to embrace palliative care, and addressing the unavoidable financial conflicts while attempting to do everything possible for a suffering patient—the issues could not have been more familiar.
As I dug through the drawers of a forgotten cabinet, I found my old slide rule.
Forty-five years ago, this instrument had been critical to my daily life and dangled from my belt for at least 2 years.
It saw steady, daily use, and with it, I was able to do complex calculations effortlessly.
I immediately tried to use it, and to my chagrin, I found I could not.
Even after searching online for operating instructions, I found it all but impossible.
Directly under this archeological stratum that had preserved the slide rule was my old, broken stethoscope—an original Leatham chest piece with the aluminum little bell broken off.
As I reverently took the remnant of the instrument out of the drawer, I remembered the process that had led to choosing this stethoscope over all the others.
Auscultation of the heart was very important for medical students.

We admired the residents and attending physician who could hear sounds that were simply not heard by us, and we rapidly learned which resident was really interested in auscultation by watching him position a patient when listening to the heart.

We also quickly learned the hierarchy of auscultation: Medical students tended to use only the diaphragm; senior residents knew how to use the bell; and cardiology attending physicians listened in both the left lateral decubitus and leaning-forward positions.
At first we learned that murmurs we couldn't hear were categorized according to 4 grades of intensity by the experts, but a new 6-grade system attributed to Dr. Sam Levine became popular.
The more secure residents joked about the grade 1 murmur, saying that only Dr. Levine and God could hear it—in that order.
Endless arguments ensued on which make of stethoscope was the best.
There were advocates for the Rieger–Bowles stethoscope.
Others with equal fervor extolled the outstanding characteristics of the Sprague–Rappaport model, and the real experts claimed the superiority of the massive three-headed Tycos model.
The newly popular Littman stethoscope wasn't even considered by any of us because none of the senior staff used it.
My problem was not merely failing to hear the murmurs but also in lacking to find an approach to cardiac auscultation.
I spent hours reading about murmurs, and the eureka moment eventually came when I found Dr. Aubrey Leatham's article about cardiac murmurs in Lancet.
Suddenly, it came to me—the reason I did not hear some of the murmurs was that I lacked the correct instrument.
I learned about the Leatham chest piece (as the stethoscope was called) from one of my tutors, and he told me that I could order this exceptional instrument from Canada.
I donated a unit of blood twice for $25 each and took the bold step and ordered the stethoscope.
In a short while, it arrived and was instantly the subject of much discussion among my classmates.
Very quickly I became a zealous promoter of the “chest piece,” and I particularly enjoyed showing off the “little bell” piece that worked well for children.
We spent countless hours taking our patients to the “quiet room,” where the triple insulation allowed us maximum concentration for the cardiac sounds.
Ever so slowly, I started to hear the murmurs others described, and I attributed this improvement to the wonderful new instrument in my pocket.
The stethoscope lasted through my housestaff training and as a young attending physician in general medicine.
In the ICU, I demonstrated to the students and residents the proper way to listen to the various anticipated murmurs and extra heart sounds.

Over the next 30 years (and two replacement Leatham chest pieces), I continued my habit of careful auscultation on rounds while my team stood at the bedside, shifting their weight from one foot to the other, urging me to stop expounding on all the sounds when they already had the echocardiography results in the chart.
During that time, I had to admit that the lengthy and careful auscultation seldom led to new findings.
Rheumatic heart disease was pretty well gone from North America, and all of the congenital heart lesions had been fixed long before the patients became adults.

There was still aortic stenosis and the description of the slow-rising pulse with an anacrotic notch, but by the time the patient arrived on the floor, there was already an echocardiogram telling us the gradient across the valve and the valve area.
Many of my colleagues asked me why I jumped through all those auscultatory hoops when echocardiography was so much more accurate.
Eventually, I was forced to admit that I really had not added anything new through auscultating the hearts of our new admissions.
Even third heart sounds failed to excite the students because the ejection fraction had been documented by an echocardiogram by the time we saw and evaluated patients with congestive heart failure.

The last time I had made a significant cardiac diagnosis was some years ago when I saw an intubated and ventilated patient with hemoptysis in the ICU, and I recognized the straightening of the left heart border with the very large left atrium on the admission chest radiograph and diagnosed tight mitral stenosis.
Yet, I will not readily abandon the process of auscultation.
I still listen to the lungs, even after I have seen the chest radiograph, and listen to the heart, even knowing the ejection fraction.
Despite recognizing that my auscultation fails to add to the care of the patient, I persist.

Touching the patient during the physical examination with my stethoscope helps to establish a connection with the patient, helps to gain their trust, and adds a bit to the often impersonal greetings our patients receive.
This impression, however, is not evidence-based.
Dad was weak and disoriented.
His skin was mottled and cool.
His blood pressure was dangerously low, and he was not making much urine.
Were his doctors and nurses paying attention?
Did they know if he was septic?
Had they left the lines in too long?
I was alarmed, but I stayed on the sideline, concerned and vigilant.
Later that morning, Dad tried to sit forward, pulling against his restraints.
He looked me in the eye and said, “Let's get out of here.”
I knew that he meant it.
I had visions of us tiptoeing down the hall, catheters trailing behind as we escaped from the intensive care unit to the farm.
I looked him back in the eye and said gently, “Dad we are going to get out of here, but this is not the time to do it.”
He settled back.
I moved to his side to comfort him, saying, “I am here with you.
You will be all right.”
As quickly as that our roles had reversed—I was now the caretaker and protector.
And then the most incredible thing happened: The roles switched back.
This very sick man looked back at me and said, “Son, I will always be with you.”
I was a son long before I became a doctor.
I should have known that these two roles would intersect someday, but I never imagined the challenges of being both a doctor and a son.
My father had grown up during the Great Depression.
“Down home” was a three-story Victorian house where he was raised by his extended family.
Uncle Ollie was a farmer and grocer.
As a child, Dad worked on Uncle Ollie's small farm.
He earned his way through law school working at Ollie's grocery.
Uncle Murrell was a policeman; and the story from my Dad was that every Christmas season Uncle Murrell and Uncle Ollie sold Christmas trees on a downtown street corner.
When Christmas Eve came, they broke out a bottle of wine and gave the trees away for nothing.
Dad had watched over his extended family as they aged and became infirm.
He brought them hams and turkeys for the holidays, visited them when they were ill, buried them when they died, and placed memorial wreaths on their graves every year.
He was the caregiver, and he watched over me.
An important change occurred the year that I graduated from medical school.
Dad bought an old farm, overgrown with trees and bushes.
He continued his law practice and pursued his passion for farming on the weekends.
I spent most of my time working as a medicine resident, but in my time off I helped him clear the land.
Finally, we planted acres of corn and a small garden with cantaloupe, tomatoes, squash, and more corn.
I completed residency training, married, and moved across the country to pursue advanced training in critical care.
I made a point of returning to the farm every year and of attending to my parents.
When Dad needed medical care I found good providers, and I took time to be there when he was a patient.
But inevitably the crisis arrived.
I had returned to the farm for a visit and while at dinner, Dad had reached for the nitro.
“Dad, tell me about this,” I said.
“Oh, just a little pain, nothing to worry about,” he said.
I knew better.
I made him promise that he would call his doctor the next day.
As I drove to the airport the following morning, I wondered whether I should have stayed, but I had long before decided to choose a doctor I trusted and let go of the urge to be my father's doctor.
The first phone call came later that afternoon.
Dad had unstable angina.
He had been admitted to the coronary care unit.
The call had been professional, reassuring, and I turned my attention to the job at hand: seeing patients, writing notes and orders, answering pages.
The next morning, the call was more alarming.
His chest pain had worsened, and he had had a run of ventricular tachycardia.
He was in the cath lab.
I struggled—should I fly back across the country now?
Not yet, I decided; and then the third call came.
The catheterization showed severe left main coronary artery disease, and my 85-year-old father was undergoing emergency bypass.
A bypass was the correct decision; but at 85, the stakes were high.
I flew home.
I had to be with Dad as his son, not his doctor.
At his bedside, the setting was all too familiar: the mechanical ventilator and diodes monitoring various vital signs.
Dad looked like so many of the critically ill patients whom I had cared for over the past 25 years.

But this wasn't just another critically ill patient, this was my Dad— the guy who watched over me, the guy I worked with to clear the farmland, the guy who let my kids drive the tractor all over the farm.
I was relieved when he wrinkled his brow and acknowledged my presence and hopeful later that night when he was extubated.
But his condition worsened the next day.
When I arrived, the nurse informed me that he had just been reintubated.
“For airway protection,” she said.
I looked at the tube in his throat.
Was he going to die?
What was going on?
Do they care?
They don't care like I do.
I saw the beginning of a long intensive care unit stay with agonies that he shouldn't have to endure at the end of a full, vigorous life.
Dad had not wanted this.
Dad's son was determined not to let this happen.
For the first time, the doctor in me emerged when a surgeon said that my dad needed a tracheostomy.
A tracheostomy would only bring depression and frustration for my father—prolong things.
“Not now,” I said, and later that day I called Dad's cardiologist and told him that we would need to talk before anyone performed a tracheostomy.
I had crossed a boundary that I had not wanted to cross.
But they would have performed the tracheostomy had I not intervened.
What would my colleagues havedone?
What would I have done in the same circumstances?
Should I have intervened?
Dad improved over the next few days.
I stayed out of medical decisions, even though it wasn't easy—decisions about weaning patients from mechanical ventilators, extubation, and removal of catheters were routine for me.
“You're better,” I told him.
Finally, they weaned him from the ventilator and extubated him.
He whispered to me that he could not take much more.
“We'll get back to the farm,” I said.
And we did.
I sat on the porch, looking out over the fields.
What happens in other families when things don't go well and there is no doctor in the family, I wondered?
Is one better off being both doctor and son—or worse?
I didn't expect the telephone conversation to take such a turn.
I was returning a call to report the results of tumor markers that were still pending at the time of my patient's clinic visit.
I told her the results, and along with them came implications I knew she wouldn't want to hear.
The tumor marker values, instead of declining in a manner consistent with a good response to treatment, were increasing.
I sensed the tension in her voice as she asked, “That's not good, is it?”
I told her that we'd need to see what the overall trend of the numbers was before we could say for certain that the disease was not responding to treatment.
I told her that the magnitude of the increase was relatively slight and explained that the imaging studies—CT and nuclear bone scans— would give us far more information.
I stated that her sense of well-being was far more important than numbers.
Then I admitted that we indeed would have liked to have seen the numbers decline rather than increase.
Her next question caught me by surprise.
“How much longer do I have?”
My mind raced as I searched for an answer.
The gulf between us seemed to widen.
I couldn't see her.
She couldn't see me.
Statistics rushed through my mind, but they were of little help.
Her words then pierced the dense silence, “I just want to be able to plan.
Because if it's just a few months, I want to quit work and spend time with my family.”
I looked at my pen.
I ran my hand along the smooth, cold surface of my desk.
“Doctors are often wrong when asked to make estimates like this,” I responded.
“I could give you the statistics, but these are just numbers, and you are a unique individual.”
“I just want to know if I should quit my job,” she half whispered.
How much longer do I have?
Her question was simple enough.
The request was reasonable.
I could give her an estimate.
How useful is that?
I looked out the window past the trees.
The yawning cerulean arc of sky above was vast.
The problem and the beauty of how this life is arranged is that there are certain things we are not meant to know with precision.
Certainly, the question of prognosis gets easier to answer when the end is very near.
But even then, we often get it wrong.
I think one of the kindest gifts we are given in this life is that we are allowed to trudge forward in complete ignorance of that which is so uniquely ours—mortality.
When and how we die is a process that unfolds in pure mystery.
When we glean some insight aided by a diagnosis or a prognosis, the passage of days becomes painfully selfconscious.
I ache with this knowledge, these collections of median survivals that bear only a vague resemblance to truth.
To think that so many upturned faces, so many searching eyes await a response from us that we can hardly provide.
Prognostication carries such weight and yet in many ways is not unlike divining the weather.
Despite the sophisticated instruments, error in forecasting is tolerated, accepted, forgiven.
I finally responded.
“I really don't know.
I think I'll have a better sense of how things are going after the next set of scans.
This is really hard to talk about with you so far away.
I can't see you or hold your hand.”
“I know,” she said.
Now I could hear the strain of tears penetrating her voice.
“It wasn't really a fair question.”
For centuries, soldiers and physicians have pondered the apparent random nature of life and death in battle.
Does fate alone dictate who lives and who dies?
Step to the left, and you survive the mortar strike.
Switch convoy vehicles, and the IED devours your friend instead of you.
Are such events predetermined?
Or do our choices decide these outcomes?
Across several combat deployments that followed the events of September 11th, every time I went to work I knew with reasonable certainty that someone would not come home.
And not just “someone,” but a patient who was also a colleague or a friend.

In my career, I've been present at the point of wounding, on the medical evacuation flight, in the aid station, and at the memorial service—a complete arc of care that made each experience of death and dying more rich and more raw.
I also often bore some responsibility for every medical outcome in advance.
Someone I knew might live, or not, because my duties as a trainer, logistician, planner, leader, or clinician were fulfilled, or not.

I wondered whether I could successfully ensure a level of care as close as possible to prevailing peacetime standards and follow the principles of best medical practice in a tactically challenging and unforgiving out-ofhospital environment.
Would life and death become more or less random based on my decisions and my efforts?
Although at times I was on the ground delivering care, other times I sat in a climate-controlled plywood room with a cup of coffee and made decisions remotely.
Would this or that person be evacuated now, later, or not at all?
I tried to be cool to both the emotional impact and the personal urgency of each caller.
I asserted real and imagined authority to remove the decision-making responsibility from the shoulders of young medics.
My conscience was a better place for these decisions to rest.

I based my decisions on many clinical and nonclinical factors—the availability or nonavailability of limited medical evacuation assets; operational challenges, such as altitude, terrain, distance, weather, security; and sometimes competing political and military interests.
My physical detachment was both easy and hard.
Like every paramedic on the street or surgeon in the operating room, I made clinical decisions with incomplete data.
Deciding from afar, via radio, telephone, or instant message, it was easy to appear cool and emotionally detached even when I wasn't.
But this same protective distance accentuated the second-guessing that naturally followed each event.
My decisions often had permanent consequences.
And having been on the other end of the call in the past, I felt sure that I knew what the men out there thought of me when I responded “no,” “not now,” or “no, not ever.”
In the end, nearly every American who entered our medical system alive remained that way.
Not so for the Afghans.
In a parallel world that collided with mine daily, my Afghan neighbors lived in a nation ravaged by more than three decades of war, blighted by collapsed political, human, and economic structures.

Carefully crafted, if not perfectly applied, medical rules of engagement, safety concerns, and limited medical evacuation platforms and hospital beds conspired to keep most Afghans out of the U.S. health care system (although a visit to any American medical facility would reveal that most patients were still Afghan civilians).

At the same time, an appropriate desire to foster self-sufficiency as part of an overall health sector development plan and a bigpicture national exit strategy further influenced clinical decision making.

Although we conducted training and development projects to improve the skills, equipment, supplies, manning, and overall capabilities of local Afghan civilian and military medical facilities, they did not provide any immediate benefit to individual patients on a given day.
Many days I denied care to Afghan patients.
Other days I treated the enemy while denying care to the innocent.
And on occasion I tended to patients with little to no chance of survival.

They would not disrupt the flow of other casualties; they might serve as useful training for my Afghan medical counterparts and my medics; and I could at least ease their suffering while I watched them die.
But I routed Afghans to their local medical facilities as much as possible, often with the knowledge that they would receive substandard care or no care at all.
How best to reconcile my Hippocratic oath with a system that necessarily operated in terms of “us” and “them,” Afghan and American, military and civilian?
Treat everyone and burden—and potentially break—a resource-limited medical system.
Treat no one and abrogate professional responsibilities.
Were some lives worth more than others?
It was a delicate balance between doing good and doing no harm.
Were my decisions right?
Were they wrong?
How much power did I truly have over life and death in my wars?
Did I make wrong decisions for the right reasons and right decisions for the wrong ones?
Would I have done the same in peacetime?
Would I do the same again?
Having left military service, I often think back on these experiences.
Questions of destiny, fate, life, and death still confound, trouble, and humble me.
I remember the names and faces of friends, colleagues, strangers, and enemies, dead and alive.
I remember good experiences, and I remember bad ones.
I remember small and large successes and failures alike.
I still practice medicine—although on many days the work seems very unfamiliar to me.
In my mind, I return to faraway places several times each day.
But except for the occasional amusing anecdote, I rarely share the details of my “former life.”
Would anyone understand?
Would they be shocked or offended?
Would they judge mefor the decisions I've made?
Worse yet, would they not care at all?
One Sunday morning, I had just completed ward rounds.
I heard a page overhead that chapel services would begin in less than 5 minutes.
My to-do list was short, so I decided to attend.
The chapel at Brooke Army Medical Center is simple, with 10 rows of chairs fanning out on either side and a row along the back wall.
When I arrived, the congregation was singing the opening hymn to organ music, and the chaplain was at the altar.
Because there were no open seats, I stood by the back entrance.
My thoughts were still focused on medical problems and many unanswered questions.
As the prayer service began, I looked up toward the altar.
Until then, I had not noticed the three young men in wheelchairs along the center aisle.
Our medical center is a busy place, providing routine and trauma care for active duty and retired military and their dependents.
Brooke has been supporting the wounded through all of our great wars.
During the Vietnam War, our hospital became famous for its burn treatment center and more recently had become an amputee center of excellence for troops coming home from Afghanistan and Iraq.
Every day I saw disfigured warriors with severe burns wrapped in neoprene masks and bandages and others with well-healed stumps after amputation.
Some wounded warriors motor around in stateof-the-art wheelchairs; others rehab on prosthetic legs or circular frame external fixators.
These images had become a large part of my hospital culture.
These images often brought me back to middle school in Philadelphia.
I had often seen many men in uniform riding in wheelchairs or walking with crutches at shopping centers, on the train, and even in Valley Forge Park.
Our black-and-white TV sets highlighted the Vietnam War every night at dinner, showing gunfire from helicopters or body bags or antiwar protesters.
The war on television had been going on since I was in the second grade.
My friends and I played all kinds of combat scenarios in the neighborhood that imitated the shows “Combat” and “The Gallant Men.”
Vietnam was a place far from where we lived.
None of my friends knew much about the war.
But seeing these college-aged soldiers without limbs made me feel so uneasy that I could not look at the amputees for very long.
My games of playing soldier became a frightening reality of war when seeing these men.
But these soldiers had lost more than a limb.
Those boyhood images never left my subconscious.
My personal intentions for attending chapel services that morning were rooted in a dedicated time for spiritual reflection; however, I became emotionally focused on the three wounded warriors.
I assessed their disabilities from the back of the chapel.
The wheelchair-bound Hispanic soldier closest to me was an average-sized guy in his early 20s.
He was reciting prayers with his friends.
Although he appeared normal, it was obvious that his legs were paralyzed.
The second wounded warrior had an above-knee right leg amputation.
He was staring straight ahead with his left foot rhythmically rocking his infant son asleep in a car seat.
This 30-something–year-old father had well-healed burns of the scalp, ears, and face, with extensive white-pink scarring melted like wax around his bony, bald cranium.
To his immediate left, his wife smiled as she held their 3-yearold daughter and proudly wore a bold red lanyard around her neck emblazoned with the letters “U.S.M.C.”
The chaplain then asked the congregation if they had any special prayer requests.
By this time, my thoughts were consumed with empathy for these disabled warriors.
The third soldier sitting near the left front row raised his hand.
He called out the names of his two buddies who had died in combat when he was injured and asked us to pray for them and their families.
The third wounded warrior, in his late 20s, was built like a linebacker with broad shoulders.
He sat at attention.
A deltoid sported a tattoo depicting a waving U.S. flag.
His left leg was elevated in a long leg cast with a 101st Airborne baseball cap resting on top of his forefoot.
His right leg was amputated below the knee.
He looked content.
His mother sat next to her son, and his father quietly wept throughout the entire chapel service.
These soldiers in the chapel unlocked the disturbing memories of Philadelphia long ago.
Their physical wounds were healing, but life as they knew it would never be the same.
Their lives had been redefined by life-altering injuries.
What had they witnessed in the theater of war?
Could they ever reconcile their wounds, pain, fears, and nightmares?
Would they ever feel whole again?
My thoughts were interrupted as the congregation began to pray.
The chaplain asked us to exchange a sign of peace with a handshake or an expression of love with a family member.
At that moment, the three young men wheeled directly toward me to shake my hand.
The macho linebacker soldier gave me a firm handshake and said, “Thanks, Doc, for taking care of soldiers.”
I sat down overwhelmed.
I had never felt so vulnerable.
I couldn't do more than sit there and cry.
followed my attending physician down the hall.
Our shoes clicked sharply against the white tile.
The space ahead opened into a crosswalk bridging the hospital towers over the street below.
Light streamed in through the large glass panels, painting the ground yellow.
White coats littered the corridor, and I glanced beyond them at the cars passing underneath.
They felt distant and small.
Inside the room, Mr. Lucas sat on the bed folding his brother's clothes.
Several suitcases lay open in the corner.
Behind him, the curtains glowed red, pulled tight to keep out the fading sun.
Pink balloons floated limply above the bed.
A bouquet lay on the desk to his right.
Mr. Lucas wasn't my patient, but I knew him well.
When his older brother had first come to the clinic with progressive chest pain, Mr. Lucas accompanied him and brought his papers in a large folder.
When he'd returned for preop screening, Mr. Lucas took notes and double-checked his brother's medications.
On the day of the operation, it was Mr. Lucas who camped out in the waiting room with blankets and a pile of books, ready for the surgeon's periodic updates.
It was Mr. Lucas who had told me about my patient's plans for the future.
It was Mr. Lucas who was there in the days following the procedure, hunched over the bed until the end of visitation hours, watching his brother's tubes rise and fall.
And it was Mr. Lucas who was here now—48 years old, with thinning gray hair and tired eyes—gathering his brother's things on his scheduled discharge day, hours after he'd died.

My attending physician told Mr. Lucas how his brother had collapsed while walking the floor, how there'd been alarms, crash carts, and people yelling for paddles as the monitor stopped dancing, and how they wouldn't know anything more definitive until after the autopsy.
Mr. Lucas sat on the bed quietly, his hands folded in his lap, eyes searching the ground for something I couldn't see.
That morning, there had been life and an expectation of more.
But that was gone now, and only words remained, like thin shells left by a receding tide.
The room was quiet.
There were no deep, heaving sobs, no quivering shoulders or staccato cries.
We stayed in the silence carefully, like three people standing over dying embers after a senseless fire, unsure of the moment and afraid.
I spent the rest of the afternoon in the crosswalk watching the street move.
The sun was setting, cutting sharp purple streaks into the sky.

Staring down past the glass, I thought about what Mr. Lucas had taught me, that medicine's “routine” tests and procedures are never routine for my patients or their families, who carry measures of anxiety and hope into each encounter; that loved ones often need as much attention and care as the patients do.

Standing there, I sensed that there would be more like Mr. Lucas and his brother, people whose stories would push me beyond the safety of clinical knowledge and technical skill into the space between medicine's great successes and vast limitations.
The possibility felt like the edge of a profoundly powerful secret.
My patients would never just be diabetics, cancer patients, or asthmatics—they'd be spouses, parents, siblings, and friends.
Those were the roles that mattered, the ones they had played before becoming my patients and the ones that would last long after.
The realization seemed so obvious that part of me wanted to laugh.
But it was also deeply convicting.
As a doctor, I would have the medications and technology, the procedural skill and expert opinion.
I would have, for all practical purposes, much of the power.

But my patients and their families were the ones who would always have the meaningful stories and goals—the linchpins holding the most challenging, frustrating, and fulfilling moments of my work in balance.
The mother worried sick about her child's ear infection.
The son unsure of how to choose death for the critically ill woman who'd given him life.
The drug-seeker and the malingerer.
The businessman concerned about a neck growth; the injured athlete who fears his career is over.
The woman facing surgery for her genetic predisposition to breast cancer.
The concerned families and friends.
And the man who collapses on the floor after crucial, life-prolonging surgery, leaving behind a brother who loved him quietly but fiercely.
In them, I would find what mattered.

Standing there with the sun against my back, I understood, deeply, for the first time since I donned my white coat and swore that sacred oath as a hopeful, wide-eyed, first-year medical student that the practice of medicine needed to be about more than correct diagnoses and management.
At best, it needed to be a long lesson in how to journey with people through crisis and into the joy and grief waiting beyond it.
It needed to exercise my heart and not just my brain.
It also needed a vision large enough to cover seasons of success and error and modest enough to find its own limitations between certainty and doubt.

And perhaps above all, it needed to inspire my own evolving story, to carry me across its long arc and change me, so that as I tried to affirm the highest values in my patients' lives, they would also affirm mine.
The thought filled me at once with hope, fear, and an abiding sense of humility.
I exhaled and felt a breath fall through my chest.
Below, the light flashed red, and the cars slowed to a stop.
Their shapes seemed closer now, blurred softly against the fading light.
I first met Marie several years ago.
She was already into her 80s and had chronic lung disease.
Her daughter, with whom she lived, hoped I could restore her strength and help with episodes of worsening cough, phlegm production, and shortness of breath.
My evaluation confirmed bronchiectasis, and together we assembled a plan for successfully managing flares with regular bronchodilator therapy and intermittent antibiotics.
A few years after I met Marie, her grandson was getting married in the South and we worked to get her ready for the trip.
We arranged for oxygen and prepared contingency plans—she did great.
In the office a few months later, I saw the beautiful pictures and enjoyed a light moment with a proud grandmother and mother.
Unfortunately, Marie declined this year.
Despite home oxygen, her health began to fail.
She told her daughter that she did not want to be hospitalized.
I asked for an office visit to be sure we had exhausted all avenues of restoring some strength.
Now with limited mobility and breathlessness, Marie relied on her son-in-law to transport her to my office in Center City Philadelphia.
Noting no other options, I resorted to a trial of corticosteroids.
Two weeks later, I checked in and learned that there had been no response.
We arranged for home care, and when she became bed-bound and began having delusions, we asked hospice to see her.
A few weeks later, her daughter informed me that she had taken a turn for the worse.
On a Thursday night, a few days after that phone message, I made a house call.
Marie's home in South Philadelphia was what is known as a “straight-through” row home—living room, dining room, kitchen, all in a line.
I entered and greeted Marie's daughter, son-in-law, and her grandson and his wife.
We exchanged small talk.
I then ascended the stairs.
Marie was sleeping in the back bedroom.
The surroundings were immaculate.
On the bedside table and dresser were pictures of her family totaling five generations.
Beside her was a glass of ice water.
She was propped on a pillow and covered by a perfectly spread comforter.
The sheets were crisp and white.
Marie was comatose but perfectly cared for, her skin free of breakdown.
She was turned slightly to the left side, soon to be turned back, as the hospice nurses had carefully instructed her daughter.
I sat in a chair and took Marie's hand.
It was cold and dusky.
Her chest revealed course rhonchi, but not an uncomfortable or audible rattle.
The atropine drops had helped.
Her cardiac tones were strong, but the pulses were faint and the pattern of respiration was clearly irregular.
On the bureau was a simple ringed notebook—the kind I used in high school biology class—summarizing Marie's care.
In its pages, I reviewed entries detailing every medication dose, periodic vital signs, observations of the hospice nurses on their visits, and notes entered by her daughter.
It was an informal medical record, yes, but one recorded with great detail, care, and devotion.
After reviewing her treatment program and the schedule of medications and care, I returned downstairs and sat with her daughter.
The wall in the kitchen was adorned with pictures and artifacts from the local firehouse where Marie's son-in-law had worked.
I enjoyed a glass of water, and we talked.
I learned more about the family as we discussed what would lie ahead for Marie in the coming days.
We reviewed who and when to call and how I would sign the death certificate.
I relaxed and enjoyed the moment just to sit with Marie's family.
I don't remember whether I stayed 10 minutes or 40 minutes, but the visit was entirely comfortable.
I gathered my things and prepared to leave.
Leaving the home, I entered the streets of South Philadelphia.
It was then that I noticed my eyes were wet.
They were not tears of sadness, though I was saddened to see Marie in the process of dying.
They were not tears of emotion, though it was an emotional situation.
They were tears of respect—respect for this patient, respect for this daughter, respect for this family, and for the profound sense of privilege to be Marie's physician.
She looked made of bones and paper.
Years of smoking had left her lungs mottled and now cancerous.
Even a few sentences left her breathing like an angry fledging lost from its nest.
She was not like this when we first met, years ago.
She was healthy and intelligent and had a smoker's cough that was not strong enough to help her quit smoking.
Even then in the beginning, i when the future was theoretical, she asked that when the time came, when her time came, would I help her?
This request was always accompanied with an intent look.
¡ I always assured her that I would.
Yes, I would help her.
Of course I would.
Hospice has been a balm to my patients, and to me, as their time came and they suffered.
I believe patients!
stories heal, and reveal, both the patient and their physician.
We physicians have those patients with whom we connect.
The connection might arise from a common inteijest like poetry or a sports team, a similar or dissimilar culture, or a shared struggle over a difficult medical illness.
With this patient, our connection was filled with humor, the clashes of our two strong wills, and her struggles with the demons of her sharp life.
It infuriated her that I was always late.
As I always do when I fall far behind, I would bring in a chocolate for her and for me.
Apologetic chocolates, I call them.
I watched her inexorable decline over months and months.
Clothes began to cling more to her frame.
Her head no longer easily tiirned because of pain and vertebral collapse.
She retained her keen sense of humor, but a certain irritability came tJD rasp between her and the world.

Increasingly, her world was limited by disease; her worst torment was boredom.j Medications, subspecialists, love of family, cigarettes—these kept her going, but the dismantling of her self continued.
I enrolled her in hospice, seeking the relief that it could provide.
She suffered the visits of the hospice nurse variably.
Opiates were ineffective and made her drowsy.
Benzodiazepines relieved her dyspnea, but she became delirious.
Her intellect was her greatest treasure, giving her access to the world oft art, music, politics, and literature.
Weighing the effect on| her consciousness, she refused further medication.
Her friends called, worried about and frustrated with her lack of comfort and frustrated with her.
"She says she's ready to die," they told me.
I expressed my sympathy and echoed their distress that she was having such difficulty.
After reviewing her past medications with the hospice team, I hoped to convince her to restart the opiates.
I felt that she would agree once I assured her that we could get her comfjjrt and ease, rest for her agitation.
For myself, I thought jof the well-established principle of double effect, which holds: If the intent of a treatment is to
O N BEING A DOCTOR keep a patient comfortable, then it is ethically allowable that death be hastened in the process.
But my patient refused this process.
She said that she was ready to die.
She did not want to spend her last days in a delirious haze.
She did not want the cognitive curtain to fall before she herself had left the stage.

I had not understood, or had willed myself not to understand, that with her intent look my patient had meant that, when the time came, she wanted me to provide her with the means to end her life on her terms.
I support the Washington Earth with Dignity Act, but I had not anticipated that I would enact such a death and not in a patient whom I had come to love.
When I became a doctor, I didn't think that I was going to be doing this.
But I didn't want anyone else to do it.
I was her doctor.
I would be her doctor carrying out her most profound wish, one that was her right, granted to her by a vote of her fellow citizens.
But I had no idea what this Death with Dignity entailed.
I wasn't sure how to start, and my ambivalence halted even an initial attempt to get started.
My patient's friends called me several times at her behest, but I remained immobile.
Resourceful, her friends explained the process to me, set the appointment for the required second opinion, and made sure that I had received word of the consulting doctor's concurrence.

As the process then wended its way on the prosaic path of forms, confirmations, waiting periods, repeated confirmations of intent, and documentation that she was not depressed, I visited with my patient several times.
She would look at me with sunken eyes tight with pain, her breathing so shallow, words barely a whisper, shake her head when I offered the pain medication.
We touched on existential beliefs, and she reassured me that she was at peace with her decision.
She didn't think that there was life after death and so—she'd nod again, say again—she was ready.
At home, I cried and thought how difficult it is to let one singular light go out.
I pondered the nature of death and that this person was the only one like her on the planet.
I thought of our relationship and all that she had given me, all that we had shared.

And then I wrote the prescription for the secobarbital that she had requested: 37 capsules to be opened, the powder stirred into the choking bitter slurry that she would have to work hard to swallow and to keep down as her last act in life.
I went to see my patient one last time.
She looked suspended in time: in bed, reduced, waiting, a book open at her side.
I sat on her bed, talking with her quietly, leaning in.
I felt tears rising up into my eyes.
She thanked me for coming.
Again, as per protocol, and perhaps for me, she repeated that she wanted to proceed with ending her life.
Then, as I held her hand, her friend came into the room and said, "Jeri is on the phone.
You asked me to call her?"
My patient closed her eyes and nodded, and the friend reached the telephone between my patient and me.
My patient separated her hand from mine and took the telephone.
I straightened up, taken aback.
I realized that the well-meaning friend had broken the plane of contact between my patient and me.
In 15 years in clinic, I can't recall a time that someone has breached that sacred space without asking me first.
And my patient had taken the telephone.
But what did I expect?
Did I expect she would say, "Can I call her back?
I'm with my doctor"?
And then I noted how her hoarse voice took new en- • ergy as she began to speak to the person on the other end of the line.
As for me, she looked up vaguely and waved me goodbye or away and went back to her call.
I turned to her friend, who thanked me with a gentle, final smile.
I stood there for another moment, or two, or three.
And then with nothing to do, I walked out of the room, down the stairs, and out onto the front porch into the bleached morning sun.
I felt bereft, at a loss, disoriented.
I realized, suddenly, that I was hot her doctor anymore.
As doctors, we say that we "lose" a patient.
But that's an illusion.
When our patients die, we lose a part of ourselves.
