That loss often happens incrementally: the diagnosis, the variable decline, more symptoms, less independence, fewer visits, then fewer telephone calls, then a final notice and perhaps a goodbye, and then we sign the death certificate and our role as their physician is over.
In my patient's choice of Death with Dignity, she was choosing to depart the medical realm.
And she was choosing to leave me—a part of that realm—as well.
As I stood there on the porch in the early morning sun, the full impact ofthat truth came upon me.
I realized, suddenly, that I was not her doctor anymore in a way more specific and acute than I have experienced before.
In a strange and painful way, I had facilitated the loss; it had been by my pen and writing on the white prescription paper that had given her the means.
But that piece of paper was just a thin last action in a much greater transition.
My patient had wrested control away from me and from the medical system.
As was her right.
I felt a pang of regret, almost embarrassment, at my resistance to recognize her truth and at my overstaying, metaphorically, at her deathbed.
Her path was her choice, her place with her friends and whatever space and time or bare earth might await her.
Mrs. Connor was a 51-year-old woman with stage IV colon cancer who was referred to our clinical trials unit after receiving extensive salvage chemotherapy.
She was hospitalized for hyperbilirubinemia and possible biliary decompression in preparation for trial enrollment.
Within seconds of meeting her, I knew that she would not be a candidate for experimental therapy.
She was severely cachectic and debilitated, barely able to sit up in bed.
Her belly was tensely swollen.
An abdominal CT scan revealed extensive ascites and hepatic metastases nearly replacing the liver.
I knew that I was about to embark on the heart-wrenching task of discussing goals of care with a woman approaching the end of her life.
I introduced myself and asked Mrs. Connor what she understood about her illness.
She accurately outlined her medical history.
I then asked about her goals.
“I want to be cured, but I guess that isn't possible,” she said.
When I asked her what else was important to her, she spoke about abdominal and leg swelling, mobility, breathing: practical quality-of-life issues.
She continued, “My family.
I love my family, and I want to spend as much time with them as possible.
I have four kids and five grandkids.
My 35-year old son, Sam, is my oldest.”
I asked whether she would like me to meet with her and her family together.
She said that she did but that Sam was the only family that she needed then.
She called Sam on her cell phone and handed it to me.
I introduced myself and asked Sam whether he was planning to visit the hospital today.
If so, I would be happy to discuss her condition with him.
When he suggested talking by phone, I concluded that he must not understand the gravity and sensitivity of the topics that I wanted to discuss.
Nonetheless, Sam was clearly the key figure in his mother's life, and I did not know how accessible he would be after I hung up the phone.
I therefore seized the opportunity and stated, “I want to discuss options for her care.”
“Are you going to put in the drain to take care of jaundice?”
“No, I don't think that will help.”
“Then what do you suggest?”
We were finally getting to the crux of the issue, but I felt uncomfortable breaking such profoundly bad news over the phone in the presence of Mrs. Connor, who had not yet heard the news herself.
“I haven't spoken to your mother about this yet, so we can speak when you get here.”
“Go ahead and tell me now.”
He had taken charge.
I am sorry to say that I think we need to stop treating her cancer and focus entirely on her quality of life.”
“Well, I have business to take care of today.
I can meet you there this afternoon.”
Sam was all business.
When I met him later that day, I extended my hand and he shook mine firmly in a businesslike manner.
“Your mother was singing your praises,” I began.
“I'm her right hand.”
“You carry a heavy load.”
“It's no big deal.
I'm used to it.”
I turned to address Mrs. Connor, “Sam obviously loves you a great deal.”
He broke in, “I buy her flowers every week.”
“I saw a picture of your kids on your mom's phone.
You have a beautiful family.”
“I worry about the little one.
“How old is he?”
“Have you spoken to him about your mother's condition?”
“Yes, in a way.”
“Has he asked about or mentioned death or heaven?”
“We can discuss ways to help you speak with him.”
It's all part of life.
He'll be all right.
He'll have to deal with it, just like I always have.
I saw my cousin die of multiple sclerosis, so I know what it's all about.
For me, everything is black and white.”
For a moment, I thought that he was referring to how difficult life was growing up as an African American.
“I had to grow up fast, take care of my family, and run my own businesses.
I don't worry about shades of gray.”
I then realized that “black and white” meant taking a stand, never doubting, taking care of business.
Sam had the drive to survive, a siege mentality.
I then asked his understanding of his mother's illness.
Like his mother, he tooaccurately described her medical history.
He ended by asking about the biliary stent.
“I am afraid that placing the drain will do no good.
There are so many tumors in the liver that draining one area will not help,” I responded.
“Then what do you propose?”
“I think that we should focus entirely on her comfort, her quality of life.
I wish that we had better treatments.”
We discussed practical aspects of optimizing her quality of life.
I then stood to leave the room.
When I did, Sam also stood and said, “Mom, I'm going to step out for a minute to get a drink of water.”
Once out of earshot, Sam got down to business.
He spoke about the details of his mother's impending death.
He explained how, at age 12, he assumed the responsibility of attending to his older cousin's daily needs as she gradually succumbed to multiple sclerosis.
He bathed her, changed her diapers and feminine pads, and did whateverelse was necessary.
He never shed a tear during our conversation.
“I had to grow up fast.
I had a job to do, and no one else was there to do it.
Now I run my businesses four days a week
so I can care for my mom the other days.”
The “black and white” theme reemerged.
“That sounds difficult,” I replied.
“It is what it is.
It's black and white.
When my cousin was near the end, she said she wanted to be buried back in Missouri, so I told her not to worry, we would take care of it.
But I knew transporting her body would cost a lot.
So, after she died, we rented a casket, bought flowers, displayed a beautiful picture of her during the funeral, and then donated her body to science so we didn't have to buy a casket or plot.
She didn't know the difference.
She was already in heaven anyway.
I plan to do the same for my mother.
She wants to be buried in Tennessee where she was born, and I told her I would take care of it.
But I have a kid in college and business expenses.
I can't afford to send her body back to Tennessee.
I would rather spend money on her now when she can appreciate it.
Like I said, I buy her flowers every week.”
As Sam spoke, he evoked the image of a lonely soldier standing sentry on a cold, dark, and ancient fortress wall, guarding against the enemy lurking in the shadows.
I realized that he stood post for his cousin who died of multiple sclerosis, guarded those walls until death overwhelmed her.
Then he stood post for his mother, who was given 6 months but was still alive a year later.
And through it all, he guarded his own family.
I was saddened by the thought of Sam promising his mother a simple white cross under an ancient oak tree in Tennessee but instead donating her body to science.
I said a silent prayer for Sam to one day be able to lay down his weapon, descend the wall, turn his face toward the sky, and breathe.
As soon as I arrived for morning rounds, the resident pulled me aside and said, “You need to talk to Chris.”
“Why?” “Mr. Simons died last night, and Chris took it pretty hard.
He was crying.”
I was attending on the inpatient medicine service of our VA hospital and had come in for Saturday morning rounds.
Chris was a medical student on the first clinical rotation of his junior year.
When he started his rotation, one of the patients he picked up was Mr. Simons.
At 6 feet, 5 inches, Mr. Simons had been a big, strong farmer.

Even into his 80s, he had been working every day and reigning as the patriarch of a large, close-knit rural Indiana family until, over several years, a series of events had turned him into a dependent invalid.
Renal cancer required a nephrectomy.
Recurrence in the other kidney required a second nephrectomy and chronic hemodialysis.
A stroke left him hemiparetic and aphasic but cognitively intact.
He became frustrated and depressed at his inability to communicate or care for himself, let alone lead his family.
His world was crashing around him, slowly, inexorably.
Hailing from rural Indiana himself, Chris became engrossed in Mr. Simons' care.
He seemed to understand and resonate with the old man and his family.
He fussed over everyday medical details before and after rounds, and it was clear that the Simonses saw Chris as “The Doctor.”
Although not fully comfortable in that role, Chris accepted it and worked hard to maximize his patients' care and minimize discomfort.
Mr. Simons had extensive arteriosclerosis, which caused not only a stroke but also multiple small heart attacks and intermittent, severe bouts of angina.
On two occasions, episodes of chest pain resulted in his being transferred to intensive care where a different set of doctors and nurses would care for him for a few days.
Although he always returned to our care after his ICU stays, such interruptions were upsetting to Mr. Simons and his family.
One day, Chris came to me and asked, “Why do we keep doing this?”
“Moving Mr. Simons to the ICU when he gets chest pain.”
“To watch him closely, treat him more aggressively, hopefully prevent a heart attack, monitor for arrhythmias, and so forth.”
“To what end?
He's going to die, sooner rather than later, and all we're doing is prolonging his suffering or even making it worse.
It really upsets him when he goes into the ICU.
They don't treat him well.”
“They don't spend time with him.
They don't try to understand him when he can't get the words out.
They do the work, but they don't seem to care for him.”
“So, what do you want to do?”
“Make him a DNR.
Don't minimize his care on the wards, but don't send him to the unit if he gets chest pain.
Don't resuscitate him if he codes.”
We agreed that we would treat his chest pain with morphine and other medications as needed, but we would not move him into intensive care, nor would we resuscitate him.
Mr. Simons and his family agreed with the plan and seemed relieved.
It was a couple of nights later, while our team was on call, that Mr. Simons died.
Chris was one of the first there and was the primary communicant with the family.
He brought the family into the room.
He then stood quietly in the corner, shedding silent tears.
I spoke to Chris after rounds the next morning and asked him what had happened.
He described the event and was satisfied that we had treated Mr. Simons well, and that his death was as peaceful as possible.
He thought the family was satisfied with his care.
“How about you?
Are you OK?”
“Yeah,” he said.
Clearly embarrassed, he added, “I kinda got emotional, too.”
Later that day, I called Mr. Simons' daughter, who had served as the spokesperson for his large, extended family.
I told her I was sorry he had passed and hoped she and her family were doing OK.
Was there anything they wanted to know from us?
“You know,” she told me, “we are a very religious family.
We believe that God takes an active hand in our lives.
We believe that everything that happens to us is done by God, for a reason.
I must say that for years, we had questioned why God was making Dad suffer so much.
The cancers, the stroke, the heart attacks, his inability to care for himself, to even talk—God was doing this to Dad, and we couldn't figure out why.
At times, we were angry at God for making Dad suffer.
But now we understand why God was doing this.
He did it to help make Chris a better doctor.”
In the dark of night in a small room in a VA hospital in the heartland, a family gathered to say goodbye to the man who had been its anchor for more than 60 years.
There were weeping, hugs, and whispers of comfort.
And there was Chris, in the corner, whose tears had given meaning to a family's years of suffering and reaffirmed their faith in God and our healing profession.
The rhythmic beat of once-familiar swing music jogged my memory.
Was it "One O'Clock Jump" or "Cottontail"?
I know I'd heard it before, many times long ago.
The radio station seldom played music of the old swing bands.

As a teenager, I had worked summers on Atlantic City's boardwalk and went every weekend to the Steel Pier's Marine Ballroom to hear the great bands—^Woody Herman, Artie Shaw, Benny Goodman, Tommy and Jimmy Dorsey, and all the others.
Did I hear it there?
Suddenly, I remembered a patient, Gladys Spink, and smiled.
It all came back.
I hadn't seen her in over 30 years.
She was a large, frumpy woman in her late 60s who always sat in the community clinic's large waiting area with her eyes shut.
I was never sure.
A diabetic and hypertensive with a long, sad face, she moved and spoke very slowly.
I originally thought that she may be hypothyroid, but her test results were always normal.
Over the 5 years she had been my patient, I seldom saw her smile.
A childless widow, she lived alone on the top floor of an old three-decker house in the neighborhood.

One day, after I had finished routinely advising her to lose weight, see the dietitian, and take her medications, I asked her, "What was the best job you ever had?"—a tactic that had led several elderly patients to open up and share a few memories of a happier time in their lives.
She paused a moment, then smiled faindy.
"Well, Doctor, I haven't thought about it for a very long time.
I hope you won't think less of me, but I was once a ballroom hostess in New York City.
Really, a professional dancer.
It was a dime-a-dance kind of place in Times Square.
My parents never approved of it.
It wasn't dignified enough.
That was the time of the big swing bands of the 40s and 50s.
You're probably too young to remember them."
"Oh no, Mrs. Spink.
A coquettish grin crossed her face.
"I even had a professional name, known to all the customers who asked for me.
Of course I didn't want to give out my real name.
It wouldn't have been proper."
"What was it?"
"I called myself Pat Allen.
"I bet you must have been very good.
Did you have a favorite dance?"
When it first came out, it was called the Lindy Hop.
That was around the time of Charles Lindbergh.
Then, later, everybody knew it as the jitterbug.
A strange name, wasn't it?
I started dancing as a teenager, like all the girls then.
You know, we were bobbysoxers with saddle shoes, pleated short skirts, and sweaters.
I decided to keep that as my uniform when I started to get paid.
The customers all liked it.
I had a special partner who came around every night—my late husband, George.
He was quite the dancer.
We even created a few steps that others copied.
Then there was the Korean War . . .
and I gave up dancing."
A mofnentary pause.
She brightened up.
"I could easily do all the over-the-shoulder acrobatic lifts, the between-his-legs sliding pull-throughs, the fast turns, and the frenzied steps.
We even won a jitterbug contest downtown once and went up to the Savoy Ballroom in Harlem to compete with the best.
Didn't win, but it was quite a thrill."
It was hard to imagine her as a "hepcat," an acrobatic jivey swinger.
I made a note in her chart and set up the usual return appointment in 3 months.
She sat in the waiting area, eyes closed, purse on her lap, with roUed-up stockings and sensible shoes.
I called out, "Pat Allen!"
She opened her eyes, blinked a few times, then smiled.
I took her arm as we walked into my office.
Before we sat down, I asked, "Come on, Ms. Allen.
I don't have a dime, but would you do me the honor of dancing with me?
Just a few steps?"
She put her purse down on a chair and took off her shoes.
"What was your favorite Lindy Hop song?"
"Do you remember Glenn Miller's 'In the Mood'?"
"Of course I do.
Let's hum it together."
Never an agile dancer, I remembered a little, a few easy twirls.
She moved quickly, gracefiilly, helping me along.
Our dance lasted only a minute or so.
I'm sure my stodgy medical school professors would have been shocked seeing us.
But then, what did they really know?
"What a nice surprise," she said.
Then we sat and discussed her recent blood tests, diet, and medications.
Every appointment after that I referred to her as "Ms. Allen."
Don't get sick in July.
This adage stems from fear that patient care at a teaching hospital in July is delivered by brand-new physicians, sometimes with more confidence than competence.
Before recent reforms in residency training, this concern was compounded by uneven oversight of attending physicians.
Once upon a July, 20 years ago now, I was that newly minted doctor.
Through hard work and serendipity, I had gained entrance to a prestigious university medical center that a friend called "the Mecca."
If you were privileged enough to serve there, your worthiness and dedication to your calling were unquestioned.
By nature, I was not one of those interns who felt that that I belonged there or that I deserved to be there.
I was filled with anticipation, excitement, and mostly . . .
How would I get through the first day/week/mo nth without hurting a patient or embarrassing myself?
I assumed that I would need help, and maybe a lot of it, from my resident, my fellow, my attending, and possibly even my co-interns that month.
Maybe a phone call to my parents for moral support would be required if things went really badly.
It had not occurred to me, though, that I may need guidance from the "less-educated" members of the team, the ones who are at the bedside around the clock.
Arrival at the Mecca commenced with orientation.
My intern class was briefed on a host of topics, ranging from the mundane (ID badges and parking) to the more serious (the mental and physical health of ourselves and our colleagues).
A parade of important people welcomed us and offered sage advice.
One instructed us on communication of all sorts.
We were advised, "Nurses are your friends.
Most have been here a very long time.
Listen to them."
At the time, I remembered it, though it did not resonate with me.
"Good advice," I thought, "but enough already with orientation."
Then, the day before our clinical rotations started, I drew the proverbial short straw.
It was so short that it was like no straw at all.
Not only was my first assignment on the challenging inpatient hematology/oneology service with its infamous reputation, but I was assigned to take call on the first day and night.
This was the only inpatient rotation where the interns were left: alone at night when the supervising resident went home once the patients were "stable."
As I soon learned, on this ward "stable" was a relative term.
Here resided the sickest of the sick, their immune systems decimated by their illnesses (think leukemia) and their treatments (think high-dose chemotherapy or bone marrow transplants).

They came from all over the region to receive treatment at the Mecca, often staying a month or more, each day with an increasing risk for hospitalacquired infections and equally deadly despair Most of the patients were "liquids": those with hématologie malignancies, such as acute myelogenous leukemia, acute lymphoblastic leukemia, multiple myeloma, and the like.

Those with solid tumors were much easier, because the chemotherapy did not devastate their bodies in quite the same way, their courses of chemotherapy were shorter, and they recovered faster and had some remaining immune ftinction.
The "liquids," however, were more taxing.
They could crash and burn at any time, and they often did.
How could I have been the first intern in my class of 31 to be subjected to this?
Didn't the program directors knowi I didn't really belong here anyway—should I tell them?
But I must have been ready, because I had my handbook.

Written for the housestaff by the truly outstanding "liquid" attendings, the handbook held between its yellow covers the basics of graft-versus-host disease, oncologic emergencies, and what to consider on the umpteenth night of neutropenic fever.
Yes, I was ready.
So, in the days before global connectivity, iPhones, and UpToDate, I had my white coat with traditionally bulging pockets, the yellow handbook, and some advice from orientation stored in my head.
About midnight on that first night, after my supervising resident had left, a nurse called me in to see a patient with leukemia who was the prototype of the medically challenging "liquid" patient.
He had developed the rigors that can accompany a fever or severe infection.
He was already receiving an alphabet soup of antimicrobials, and he had no white cells.
He looked sick, both chronically and acutely, and it scared me.
He scared me.
The nurse asked me what I wanted to do for this.
As I helplessly watched his discomfort, I felt that the nurse was watching as the blood drained from my face.
She calmly said, "This is common when people are getting ampho."
She nodded toward his IV pole where his now-empty bag of amphotericin hung.
I nodded, looking at her expectantly.
Surely, she had worked on this unit at least since I was in kindergarten.
She continued, "Usually we use Demerol to treat this.
Would you like to order that?"
Relief washed over me.
And she continued, "Given his size and kidney fianction, 50 mg will work."
"Okay, please give Demerol, 50 mg IV."
As the rigors ceased, both my patient
and I enjoyed a reprieve.
The rest of the night, he remained comfortable.
He was "stable."
And I was learning.
The days and nights that followed brought more calls and bedside evaluations, many more serious than that one.
But the tone had been set, at least in my view.
The nurse's calm demeanor and respectful communication were not lost on me.
Calm is good.
Offering, asking for, and accepting help are wonderftil tools and can be done without demeaning a colleague.
And although our education as physicians provides a unique medical expertise, -we can all learn something from an experienced nurse.
These lessons continue to serve me well in my current work as a hospitalist.
The nurses save me in.
some way every day, with their humor, their respect, their attention to detail, and their clinical acumen.
But the first time that I remember a nurse saving me, in that brief encounter, I understood that nurses are my allies and have much to teach me.
Twenty years later, they still aré and they still do.
I try to thank the nurses every day.
Especially in July.
I did my grieving in the days following my father's diagnosis.
Just 2 weeks after the birth of my first child, a son, and his only grandchild, I was in no place emotionally to begin a headlong dive into mourning, but that is exacdy what happened.
When he handed me his CT scan report—pancreatic mass, liver métastases, and lytic bone lesions—I knew that Dad had litde time.
I work as an obstetrician near the medical facility where Dad started treatment.
He allowed me to go with him to the first appointment.
Leaving bottles of pumped breast milk at home with my husband, I accompanied my parents to meet the doctor and begin their long journey into living with terminal cancer.
After I broke down in tears when the doctor asked me how we all were doing, I was banned from future appointments.
Dad didn't want me to hear any bad news.
To him, I was his daughter, not a doctor.
It didn't occur to him that I already knew the worst.
I already knew how it would end, and I had already started to mourn.
On that first visit to the oncologist.
Dad took a litde piece of paper on which he'd written some specific questions.
Even though he never let me see that list, I knew that one of the questions, the one he didn't say out loud in front of me was, "How long do I have?"
His physician patted him on the shoulder and said that they'd get started with chemotherapy first and discuss prognosis later.
Dad spent hours dissecting Internet articles that described stage rV pancreatic cancer, but he never had an open conversation with his treating physician about the natural history of his disease.
I remembered theories of grief from medical school, and I watched as Dad bounced back and forth between denial and anger.
He tried to convince himself that his pancreatic cancer was somehow different from what he read about online.
He became angry when no one, including me, would espouse the theory that Zocor was the cause of his disease.
Pricklier than ever, he lashed out at my mom for the smallest things, and I tried to comfort her by explaining that this was his attempt at coping.
All the while, I prayed he would realize that there wasn't any time to waste on pettiness.
I spoke with hini carefully, trying to discern the extent of his insight, and answered him honestly, but I was unable to help my father understand he was going to die.
I had faith that his oncologists, stewards of the dying, were going to walk him through that difiBcult revelation.
Both of us, my father and I, were mistaken in our expectations.

When he was a young boy, my dad considered becoming a physician, but one summer his father, a physics professor, took him to the Princeton University campus where Dad saw the beautiful buildings and decided to devote his life to creating monuments.
Dad was a brilliant architect, dedicated to the details, pouring over Vitruvius, and aspiring to Palladio.
He studied his clients with an equal fervorgetting to know them personally and connecting on a deep level, all while performing his art with intellect and honesty.
He would have been an exceptional doctor.
Struggling financially to provide for our family, I always wondered if he regretted choosing architecture over medicine.
He was just interested enough and just intelligent enough about medical principles to be dangerous and even annoying at times (hence the Zocor obsession).
Although he admired doctors, he was also skeptical that they had their patients' best interests in mind.
This mistrust was explicit at the end.

One evening I was sitting with him as he lay in bed after a particularly exhausting series of chemotherapy treatments and procedures, during which another week had passed without seeing or talking to his doctor.
He could barely speak as he was so tired, but he voiced to me his disappointment that his treating physician never called to check up on him.
I tried to make excuses, saying how busy he probably was, how many people he served, how maybe he didn't realize how desperately ill Dad was feeling.
But my father was so angry, and unfortunately, it's one of the few conversations from the last few months that will be forever engraved on my memory.
"These doctors," he said to me, his doctor-daughter, "What sons of bitches they are."
Tears were coming to his eyes, and mine.
"They don't even call you to check on you when you feel like you're dying."
Even as a lowly architect, he continued, he was constantly calling his clients, past and present, to just to say, "Hello."
He could not comprehend how the medical professionals, especially oncologists, did not do the same.
It was the only time I called his doctors, and I spoke to the nurse practitioner, apologetic for the intrusion but asking if they could call my dad at some point just to check in on him.
She was sympathetic, and she called him later that week.
Dad was appreciative but still felt neglected by his doctor and justified in his cynicism.
Maybe it is true that some people never graduate from a certain stage of grief and that no matter where the grieving person is, that is where others should allow him to be.
I couldn't help wondering though if a lack of open communication with his physician hindered Dad's process of letting go.
He would get angry with us when we asked him how he was feeling.
He'd take our hands and place them on his bony shoulders and say, "How do you think I'm feeling?"
As he grew weaker, thinner, sicker, he asked again, this time of another physician, "What are my chances?"
That doctor replied that Dad had a 50/50 chance.
"What do you mean 'a 50/50 chance'?"
I asked my mom when they came home with this latest report.

I was gende but honest with her in explaining that the statement made no sense, but she felt better holding onto even the thinnest strand of hope while I felt the burden of Dad's diagnosis grow heavier.
After each doctor's visit, as Mom would relay word for word what was said, I became more frustrated.
My father was wasting away before our eyes, and he knew it, but no one, not one provider, took his hand and dared to face the anger and sadness with him.
He even told me that the doctor had stopped touching him at his visits.
The doctor used to feel his belly, increasingly distended with fluid ahd tumor, or pat his shoulders to feel the lack of muscle mass, but now he just breezed in, said a few things, and was gone.
The result was that my father never got prepared.
One week before he died and 6 months into treatment, he was admitted to the hospital after his weekly appointment because he was too weak for Mom to get him to the car.
His doctor was out of town at the time, but during the overnight admission, one of the young doctors in the group finally had a conversation approaching reality with Dad.
He explained in a kind but straightforward way that the chemotherapy had not worked and they needed to stop treatment.
He recommended that Dad go home with hospice.
That night I stopped by the hospital room after my shift was over.
I was nervous to see him after this conversation because I thought it might escalate his defensiveness.
Instead, he had more relief on his face than I had seen in months.
He actually smiled at me and took my hand when I came to the bedside.
Finally, it was out of his hands.
Finally, he could rest.
By this point there was no time to get things ready.
Once we took him home with hospice, it was just a matter of days before he was, gone.
The last thing he said to us was, "It wasn't supposed to be this way.
I thought I had • more time."
Two days after his death, we received a copy of the death certificate, signed by Dad's physician who had been caring for him the last 6 months of his life.
There was never any phone call, never any card.
It was like his time in treatment had never happened.
Maybe, I reasoned, this is how it's handled in the real world, even though I had done more for my patients with cancer as a busy gynecology resident.
Facing the shortcomings of my profession and how much it disappointed my father and my family when it.
mattered most compounded the grief I felt in Dad's death.

Every day as I drive by the hospital where Dad was treated, I am disturbed by the thought that what our family experienced is being repeated daily in a medical culture that avoids guiding patients and their families through these difficult dialogues.
So many dollars spent and so many papers written on genes and therapies' that may, possibly, lead us closer to a cure.
But the physician's arts of compassion, brave words, and gentle touch has never eluded us.
To the terminally ill and the families they will leave behind, there may be no treatment more important than these.
Many physicians excel in meeting patients in their grief and they struggle right alongside them until the end.
Other physicians, including those who cared for my father, do not.
They should do better.
Let's make ourselves da better, so that our profession is known for its humanity as well as its science.
And if that's not what we're striving for, then maybe all of us should have chosen architecture instead.
To prepare the mind for medicine, nothing surpasses bird watching—birding, to its active practitioners.
Both birding and diagnosis demand and reward many of the same traits.
Each vocation brings its world into sharp focus and provides endless variety.
To study birds and their migrations is to have one's finger on the pulse of the planet.
The ICD-9 and the world's avifauna each contain nearly 10 000 entries—if you include such terms as “accident involving spacecraft” and “diabolical nightjar,” respectively.
Birds and infections achieve dazzling and confounding diversity in the tropics.
Each may gain latitude and altitude with climate change.
Both invite endless discovery, the occurrence of each bird and malady often routine but occasionally stunning.
Common things being common, identifying both birds and disease requires a basic understanding of probability.
They are easier to identify in context, either bird or rash from a photo more challenging than in the field.
Some gulls and shorebirds require age determination before they can be named.
Gender matters, too—many birds and maladies are classically described by features found in males.

Lest we forget that coronary disease in women may present without angina, a female scarlet tanager is green, and a female black-throated blue warbler is neither black-throated nor blue.lent migrants with vocal resident species in the autumn woods, like vascular disease with diabetes.

I think often of Sam, who was visiting his daughter while she was undergoing chemotherapy, when an unprovoked thrombosis with mild anemia announced his own silent stomach cancer the way clamoring crows betray a sleeping owl on its daytime roost.
Variably confiding and secretive, some healthy birds and less healthy humans are more active at night.
You will detect and identify a lot more birds and illnesses if you listen.
In the forest, many birds are first detected by sound—if at all—and some visually identical species are distinguishable only by their voices.
Diagnosis without the history is like birding without the song.
It is said that the patient will tell you the diagnosis if given the opportunity.
One patient of mine did so with improbable precision.
During treatment for a urinary tract infection, she presented with rapidly worsening dyspnea.
Her chest radiograph showed diffuse infiltrates, but her symptoms did not suggest pneumonia or congestive heart failure.
After presenting her with the inconclusive results of various studies and assuring her that we would keep at it until we figured it out, I asked if she had any ideas to explain her symptoms.
“I reckon it was that antibiotic they give me,” she replied immediately.
Meaningful glances and faint smiles among our team surrounding her bed gently dismissed her naive theory.
Then came a moment of silence as we regrouped in the hall, humbled by the realization that she had nitrofurantoin lung.

To bird or doctor well requires keen eyes and ears for detail: alert to the distant wheeze of a gnatcatcher in the treetops or the narrowed airways in a barrel chest, the tinge of yellow in a jaundiced eye or in the throat of a “little brown job” in the underbrush, the faint shadow across the face of a thrush or of a young woman with fatigue and arthralgia.
Pivotal field marks are said to be diagnostic.
You have to tune out background noise to hear the unfamiliar song, to suppress pager and schedule to listen to a new patient's story.
One approaches each individual with an open mind, to notice deviations from the pattern.
The third sandpiper from the left feeds with a different posture; this fellow with a backache can't get comfortable at night.
The more experience with common varieties, the easier it is to spot exceptions, provided you don't become jaded and stop paying attention.
Of recognizing both strange bird and rare or subtle disease, chance favors the prepared mind.
Pattern recognition is often easy—a flamingo or an asthma flare—but sparrows and delirium require some discernment.
To an experienced observer, the briefest glimpse of a passing hawk, a single call note in the dark, pursed lips, or pill-rolling tremor in a crowd may afford diagnostic certainty.
On the other hand, to identify a Nutting's flycatcher, it helps to see inside its mouth.
A silent Empidonax and a mute, ill elder both mock my abilities.
Some elusive birds and symptoms must be let go unnamed (or perhaps NOS), but another quiet walk through the same patch of woods or data may still yield the answer.
Surgeons sometimes say the intense focus of the operating room shuts out all other concerns; so, too, the concentrated view through a spotting scope.
The radiologist wants a better angle, the dermatologist better light, the birder both.
Physicians, at least, are usually unhindered by foliage or weather conditions.
Birders have the advantage, though, of observing their subjects in their natural habitats.
Both prone to obsession, doctors and birders in groups share a boundless capacity to bore nonmembers of their learned societies.
Both seem to thrive on the stories they accumulate.
There are the intellectual and altruistic—for doctors—rewards of “getting it,” but also the distinct ego gratification of having gotten it.
A “good case” and a “good bird” have similar meaning, both subject to correct identification and to peer review.
You don't want to be either one.
Birds far beyond their physiologic boundaries may not find their way home alive, either.
Both pursuits require attention, patience, mindfulness, and clarity; both are fraught with uncertainty and subject to human error, a birder's embarrassing at worst.
He was handsome, with dark, defined eyebrows and long black bangs on smooth olive skin.
He was clearly in pain, but he refused to cry.
By the definition in his biceps and the “cool” swagger of his gait, I guessed he was about 20 years old.
He came in clinging to a simple white envelope in his left hand; his right hand was wrapped in a bundle of dirty towels.
It was a slow afternoon in the emergency department, so several doctors and nurses were available to ask him basic questions: “¿Que´ estaba haciendo cuando ocurrio´?
¿Cua´ntos an˜os tiene?”
It happened while he was working in a factory.
Yes, he was in a lot of pain.
And his age?
Like other patients who arrived in the emergency department at this large public hospital in Honduras, he sat on 1 of 2 vinyl examination tables.
The room smelled of blood from previous patients—machete wounds, gunshots, car accidents.
The clean-up crew consisted of an elderly woman and a dirty mop.
No paper barrier on the examination tables.
Despite the pain in his hand, he was surprisingly calm and composed.
A nurse removed the towels to expose the wound.
Suddenly, it looked like a scene from “The Itchy & Scratchy Show”— his thumb was gone, and an artery squirted blood in synchrony with the rhythmic pulse of his heart.
The fully exposed bone distorted his hand's anatomy.
The doctors, excited to have visiting medical students from New York City, urged us in for a closer look.
they encouraged, perhaps hopeful that a positive experience in their hospital would lure us back to Honduras later in our medical careers.
The boy became woozy and had to lie down while the doctor cleaned the wound.
With a glimmer of relief, he passed his cherished envelope to the doctor.
The doctor took a quick glance, shook his head, and tossed it back on the table.
The boy appeared confused.
Why weren't they preparing his thumb for reattachment?
His face grimaced in despair.
The doctor finished washing the wound with antiseptic and sewed the remaining flaps of skin over the stump.
I felt disheartened and powerless.
As a first-year medical student, I still identified more with the patient than with his doctors.
I was uncomfortable in his silence; why wasn't he questioning his doctors?
I wanted to explain to him what was happening and why, but I didn't have the language skills or medical expertise to communicate effectively.
I wanted to fix all the circumstances and inequities that landed him in this situation.
I was frustrated the rest of that afternoon.
Suddenly, the muggy heat and flies vexed me.
I whined about having to use my personal hand sanitizer because soap was not readily available in the hospital.
I grimaced as doctors opted not to use gloves when patients had only minor bleeding.
And I watched detached doctors socializing while treating patients in unbearable pain with life-threatening trauma wounds.
The boy had given me a new awareness of how health care in this poor, underserved community could change a person's life, and I couldn't stop comparing care in his world to care in mine.
Since leaving Honduras, the troubling health disparities between our worlds continue to preoccupy me.
The boy worked hours away from the hospital, and neither he nor his coworkers knew that amputated body parts need to be kept cold to remain viable.
(This, however, was a moot point, because the hospital was not equipped for neurovascular reattachment.)
That wouldn't happen where I'm from.
In fact, none of this would happen where I'm from.
In Paradise Valley, Arizona, 14-year-old children don't have occupational hazards because they don't have occupations.
And if they do suffer accidental injuries, they have quick access to medical care.
And they have families to advocate on their behalf.
And they are in a country with the technology and resources to perform surgical wound debridements, skin grafts, and neurovascular reattachments.
As a surgical patient during my teenage years, I got care that a 14-year-old boy in rural Honduras could never dream of receiving.
But then, so did my friend's dog, Steeler.
My friend adores his dog and provides him with a lovely life—it's a running joke among our friends that we'd all like to be reincarnated as Steeler.
So when Steeler was hit by a car a few months ago, it wasn't surprising that my friend took an overnight flight from India to the United States, despite the cost of a lastminute ticket.
Steeler wasn't in good condition: He was in the doggie ICU (DICU, as my friends joked), was incontinent, and was paralyzed in his hind legs.
So the dog stayed in the DICU for a few weeks, receiving 24- hour care, intravenous pain medications, and a catheter.
A CT scan showed an intact spinal column despite broken vertebrae.
With each e-mail update about the dog's health, I grew more uncomfortable—I thought back to the conditions in Honduras and the boy who received less care than a dog in the United States.
Meeting the boy in Honduras left me with a host of questions about my future role as a physician.
The Honduran doctors' limited goal of patient stabilization isn't bad medicine—it's the best they can do with their resources.
But how do I resolve this lack of social justice?
How will I narrow the divide through my career?
Still early in medical school, I'm accustomed to facts and clearly defined answersto guide me.
But here, there are no multiple-choice answers to offer clean resolution.
I think about the boy in Honduras often.
When I left the Honduran hospital that steamy afternoon, I saw him wandering in the dirt outside, seemingly lost.
I wondered what he would do without his right thumb.
How would he work and support himself?
How would his family and community respond to his injury?
Would he be less desirable to women?
Would the “cool” swagger of his gait be permanently lost?
I still wonder.
Recently, I was assigned to care for a 15-year-old girl with ataxia telangiectasia, the debilitating neurodegenerative disease that lacks an adequate DNA repair mechanism.
The girl's documents revealed a history of repeated hospitalizations for pulmonary infections.
Most recently, during a 10-day course of unremitting pneumonia, ciprofloxacin-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa was cultured from her sputum.
This time, the patient's symptoms included a temperature up to 39.5 °C accompanied by greenish productive cough, shortness of breath, and pleuritic chest pain for the past 3 days.
On auscultation of her lungs, I noticed decreased breath sounds on the right, and wet rales could be heard diffusely.
Routine blood tests supported an infectious origin.
With ataxia telangiectasia being a cancer-prone genetic condition, the use of ionizing radiation was to be restricted.
We opted to do chest radiography only when absolutely necessary rather than to provide a baseline cardiothoracic window.
Yet, even without imaging to confirm our presumptive diagnosis, pneumonia was the most reasonable explanation for her symptoms.
We started therapy with the same medications that had worked for the patient during her last hospitalization, but to cover atypical organisms, we also sent a sample of her sputum to the laboratory.
While hoping for a response to antibiotics, I went to the library.
A Science article from 1995 announced an exciting research finding.
The discovery of a single gene, ATM (ataxia telangiectasia mutated), that may underlie the pathogenesis of the disease was regarded a major breakthrough.
I was curious to learn what clinical implications had been created by biological research into the ATM gene in the 15 years since its identification.
I began to extract relevant articles from MEDLINE.
Several papers focused on ATM inhibitors as novel radiosensitizers that may better target cancer cells.
Other than these, however, most citations linked to basic research that was still remote from any practical utilization.

The sequencing of the gene might have been the culmination of a virtuous, creativity-laden research project, yet its promise for patients suffering from malicious neurodegenerative diseases had not even begun to be fulfilled.
I returned to the department disappointed.
A few days later the girl's cough gradually began to subside, her fever abated, and her chest discomfort diminished.
Still, her mother mentioned that the patient had an irritating pain located near her lower ribs.
We dismissed it as secondary to her contained pulmonary infection.
Antibiotics should alleviate the infectious process, and the discomfort would inevitably fade, we reassured the worried mother.
On morning rounds, we performed a brief assessment but invested the lion's share of the time on scholarly topics.
We discussed fetoprotein, a useful serum marker for ataxia telangiectasia.
We reviewed the recent surge in Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia among immunocompromised patients.
We reviewed the vast array of antimicrobials, discussing the latest alternatives to the standard antipseudomonal regimen.
Always, it seemed, we favored the peculiar over the familiar, covering a dozen topics but never the very necessary procedure of clinical assessment.
Tactile fremitus and resonance failed to make it into our bedside symposium.
There was no exception taken to our bedside agenda— that is, except for one helpless voice.
After the rounds, the girl's mother would approach me and repeat her concerns, reminding me that our theoretical debates had not altered her daughter's serious condition.
All I did was to promise to wield all the knowledge and available means for the benefit of her daughter.
The next morning, we realized that the lower rib pain had become the poor girl's nightmare.
Her breathing became labored, and her posture was almost twisted in an attempt to reduce the severe pain.
Chest radiography became a vital necessity, and the images were strikingly abnormal.
A huge pneumothorax threatened to compress the mediastinum and shift the opposite lung.
We called the pediatric surgery department immediately.
The girl was transported to the pediatric intensive care unit, where a chest tube was inserted.
The procedure was successful, and she recovered within a few days.
Sometime later, I participated in a bedside tutorial in our cardiology department.
The cardiologist emphasized some very basic physical examination tools.
From head to toe, he encouraged us to exploit every physical sign to aid in diagnosis.
He joked that a medical student's lung percussion skills may roughly correlate with his or her cumulative period on the wards.
His advice struck a deep chord with me.
I recalled my own omission of basic clinical skills in my patient with ataxia telangiectasia and the concealed pneumothorax.
The traditional physical examination should have come first and would have served me well, as it would have the patient and her mother, whose supplemental history I had ignored.
This case, in the age of high technology and molecular biology, brought home to me the importance of first principles: the history and physical examination.
You can't imagine what you can know about your patients,” my quality assurance director began.
We were sitting in my office side by side, staring at the computer together.

After long months of grading my quality performance and suggesting ways to improve my scores, the director found some time to help me develop more intimate relations with the BINA (intelligence) quality control computer program.
“This program will enable you to analyze, compartmentalize, categorize, and integrate data in a way that will tell you everything about your patients,” she promised.

“You will also see why they aren't balanced in terms of weight, blood pressure, cholesterol, glucose—and you'll see why they adamantly refuse to come even close to reaching the numbers they should be reaching on all those measurements.
You and the other family doctors said that each case is its own unique story.
You protested that the statistics made you look bad.
To address this, our best minds came up with this program, where you can assess each case on an individual level.
Believe me, when you try this, you will find it absolutely wonderful!”
On she went.
“Let's take a look at this patient.
According to the table, he has four risk factors with a high LDL cholesterol level.”
I squirmed in my chair when the system quickly pointed out Sammy's four risk factors: male, 54 years old, smoker, and high blood pressure.
His LDL was 157.
What a disgrace!
I was ashamed.
“I prescribed statins,” I muttered, “and I even increased his dosage when he didn't reach the target levels.”
The quality assurance director continued to perform her magical data-collecting stunts.
“Let's review the system monitoring his medicine distribution.
Okay, here we can see that over the last 3 months he has taken only one prescription, on average, for each of the five medications he regularly receives!”
“The medicine distribution system receives realtime input from all of the pharmacies that have agreements with the HMO.
This way we can assess the patient's compliance!”
I let out a heavy sigh.
I knew Sammy very well.
Every morning at 5 a.m. he arrives at the market in the city, selling parsley and basil from his very own fields.
On Fridays, he sells flowers.
In the afternoons, he works as a handyman in peoples' homes.
Just this week, Sammy told me his most beloved daughter was getting married, and he wanted to buy her a nice gift.
Sammy also told me with a proud smile that every month he takes only some of his medication, saving the money for other necessities.
Last month there was an oil leak in his old car that he uses to bring his goods to the market every day.
Two months ago his wife had a tooth infection that required a root canal and a cap.
And at the end of August, he had to buy a nice new backpack for his eldest grandchild who was going into the first grade.
He seemed quite pleased with his method.
“You prescribe five types of pills: three for blood pressure, one for cholesterol, and another one for heartburn.
Every time I go to the pharmacy I tell them, 'Don't give me that one this time.
I have a whole package at home.'

But the truth is, I take the pill for my blood pressure once every two days, the little pink pill for heartburn I take when it really hurts, and the cholesterol pill only when I'm not so tight for money.”
He is practically beaming as he recounts his self-management system, almost as bright as my quality assurance director is with her new system that seems to know everything about him.
After we agree that I need to speak with Sammy about his treatment compliance, another interesting piece of data catches her eye.
Sarah—an 82-year-old woman with diabetes.
She hasn't visited an eye doctor in 5 years!
Hemoglobin A1c, 7.9.
Her microalbumin–creatinine ratio has not been checked in over a year.
Not monitored, not balanced.”
She doesn't explicitly say that I am a horrible doctor, but I can feel the disappointment in her voice.
In my mind's eye I travel to Sarah's small house in the village.
She is a thin, upright woman, nearly transparent by now, with a mound of white hair that endows her with a majestic appearance.
Sarah lives on her own, surrounded by paints, decorated ceramic bowls, and clay birds of her own production.

Every holiday she brings me a ceramic salad bowl, a plate to hang on the wall, or something she created at the elderly citizen's club, often painted in blues and always so innocent, with a carefully and neatly applied signature on the back.
Sarah is a Holocaust survivor.
She was in Auschwitz.
She survived and immigrated to Israel, raised a small family, and tended to their modest farm.
Sarah's son died of lymphoma in his early 20s.
Her husband died of a heart attack 1 year later; they said he died of “heartbreak.”
Her daughter gave birth to her only grandchild, who lives in the big city.
Sarah's daughter died young as well—of breast cancer.
Now, Sarah is completely alone, dying of lung cancer after having smoked for many years.
I haven't been tracking her diabetes for months.
We speak of so many other things.
Will she live long enough to see the rosebuds flower in her garden?
Will she have enough strength to hold a paintbrush for a few consecutive minutes?
Will she manage to finish sculpting the candlesticks she is now working on to sell them at the elderly citizen's fair before the high holidays?

In a confidential tone, Sarah shares that her granddaughter is pregnant, and she wants to have the chance tohug her great-grandchild, after decades of loneliness and longing for her deceased loved ones.
She shows me the envelope she keeps in the drawer of her bedside table, next to the oxygen device and the piles of narcotic medicines.
I know that inside that envelope lies her simple will, in which she bestows her small house to her only granddaughter.
Words such as “eye doctor” and “microalbumin” have not entered our conversations for some years, and I'm not sure I regret that.
The quality assurance director continues to scan the tables and charts.
“Oh, here's Eli.”
She marks his row in yellow.
“Male, age 45, LDL 240, and he smokes, with a family history and very high risk.
Medicines—he doesn't have any prescriptions.
Perhaps you should invite him in and offer him a statin.
You agree with me that these are unequivocal indications for such treatment?”
I agreed with her, indeed.
I had invited Eli to come in yesterday.
I invited him last week as well.
I even invited myself to visit him at his mother's home where he resides to encourage him to start taking care of himself.
I gave him brochures, with pictures of vast green lawns, flowers, exercise rooms, and arts-and-crafts rooms at some of the best rehabilitation centers in the country.
Eli is an alcoholic, as was his father.
I treated his father throughout the months of his deterioration from liver cancer.
Eli is a charmer, with sad, almond-shaped eyes.
Ten years ago, when he worked as a chef in a small country restaurant, he used to bring me his freshly baked walnut bread or a jar of pesto sauce with an unforgettable aroma.
Eli was a talented cook and host, and a pleasure to talk to.
He now begins every morning with a bottle of vodka, ending every long day in a haze of anger, vomit, and self-loathing.
He sometimes appears in my office emanating a horrid odor, totally despondent.
He knows I remember him from his better days.
He sits silently and cries.
His mother comes more frequently, asking if I have succeeded in reaching his heart and convinced him to begin rehabilitation.
He doesn't want to go.
He can stop whenever he wants.
He also doesn't want medication—he doesn't believe in it.
He believes in a healthy lifestyle, spiritual and clean.
One day—when he has the strength.
The quality assurance manager awakes me from my wandering thoughts.
“Our up-to-date online system can provide you with so much essential information about your patients.
You only need to look at it, and the whole story is already there.”
Yes, there are many things I can know about my patients to help improve their health measurements, if only I didn't spend so many hours listening to their stories.
Each one leaves a deep imprint on my heart, a story that cannot be quantified through numbers alone.
But I smile apologetically to the manager and promise to improve my ways in the future.
While it thundered and rained outside one Thursday evening, I sat comfortably in my office reading a new report on TB.
According to the article, the incidence of TB has increased recently in China, especially in the elderly population.
My mobile phone rang.
It was Dr. Li, a physician and good friend from my hometown.
After catching up, his voice grew serious.
He told me that his lifelong friend, Mr. Liu, had been coughing up blood, and there was a lesion in his upper right lung on the chest radiograph.
He thought it could be cancer, although he hoped there could be another explanation.
I immediately suggested TB to break the uncomfortable silence.
He disagreed because Mr. Liu is 65 years old, but I quickly brought up the article I was reading about TB in China.
He suggested that I evaluate Mr. Liu, and I graciously agreed to help my old friend's friend.
After viewing the x-ray, I was almost certain it was cancer, but I kept this to myself.
More work needed to be done to be certain, so I referred him to my friend, Dr. Fan, an oncologist.
His clinic is in the building next to mine, and I took Mr. Liu through the garden to get there, which he enjoyed.
When we arrived at Dr. Fan's department, his smile changed to a frown.
The space consisted of row upon row of beds, crowded with patients and their relatives.
After passing through the long corridor lined with extra beds, we came to Dr. Fan's office.
He was busy drawing marks on the face of a patient.
I could tell this was a patient with nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
After drawing on another 4 patients, he washed his hands and took out a pack of cigarettes from the upper pocket of his white coat and handed one to me.
I immediately noticed the red color of the box, not the usual and most popular blue brand used by the doctors here.
The red is an obvious sign of social status, as it is more expensive and rarely seen in daily life.
In China, sharing cigarettes with someone is a sign of friendship and respect.
It is therefore often difficult to refuse the offer, even for a nonsmoker.
As a result, it is a very common practice to share cigarettes among doctors themselves, and even among doctors and patients.
About 45% of male doctors in China smoke.
Only a small number of doctors advise their patients to quit smoking.
Having recently returned from abroad, I am still not used to the custom.

With the cigarette in my right hand, I wondered whether it was appropriate to smoke in the office and especially in front of a patient like Mr. Liu, who I suspected had lung cancer, the disease often caused by smoking.
Dr. Fan stretched his right hand toward me with his lighter.
I put the cigarette between my lips and let him light it.
He then lit one for himself.
Embarrassed, I could feel my face turn red.
I took a glance at Mr. Liu and his wife, standing in front of the office door, waiting quietly for us to examine Mr. Liu's radiograph.
Surprisingly, I didn't notice any change of expression on their faces; it seems that they were used to watching their doctors smoke in front of them.
I relaxed as we started to examine the chest radiograph through a veil of smoke, enjoying our friendship and a feeling of harmony.
“I am almost sure this is cancer,” Dr. Fan said to me as he pointed to the lesion on the film.
“Of course we still need the pathologic confirmation,” he added.
“Can it still be removed surgically?”
I asked him.
The answer was no.
The biopsy was arranged for the following Tuesday.
On the way back to my office, as we passed through the lush garden again, Mr. Liu said to me, “Please tell me the truth when the pathologic results are available.
I am not afraid of death.”
On Sunday morning, while I was preparing slides for a meeting, Mr. Liu called me and asked whether he and his wife could come and have a chat.
Sitting in front of me, he began his story.
He was born in the old society when the Chinese Nationalist Party was ruling China.
He told his story like a guest teacher coming from a factory or farm to give political lectures to secondary school students—something I have experienced many times before.
“I quit school and started to work very early in my life and overcame a great deal of painful events, as many people did in my generation,” he said quietly.
He told me that he once worked as a teacher in a primary school and later became an accountant in his village.
Then he was promoted to a department manager of a reservoir.
He soon lost that job, however, in the political turmoil.
Without a profession, life was hard.
But since last year, he received retirement pay.
Because his working years were determined from the first day he started to work, he is now paid well and even has some extra to save.
He looked at his wife, who sat next to him listening to his story silently, and he said, “But she does not have any income and has to rely on me now.
I do not know what she is going to do if I die.”
His wife started to sob.
Without the pathologic results, we are not 100% sure if it's cancer.
Maybe it is just TB,” I told him, hoping it would console her too.
Cancer or TB?
Which one will be more likely?
I asked myself.
“Have you ever smoked?”
“Yes,” he gave me an honest answer.
“How many cigarettes do you smoke each day?”
“Only 1 pack a day.”
“How many years have you smoked already?”
“About 30 years.”
A pack a day for 30 years surely had cost him a lot of money, considering the lack of a steady income until a year ago.
Unfortunately, this is exactly the cause of his lung cancer, I thought to myself.
“I know someone who has lung cancer, but has never smoked,” he broke the silence.
I wanted to tell him that about 90% of the lung cancer patients are current or previous smokers and that smoking is a major cause of lung cancer, although not an exclusive one.
Other factors have certainly been involved in the initiation and development of lung cancer.
But it was not the right time to talk about the causes of lung cancer, so I stopped myself and just answered him with a smile.
When the pathology report arrived, it was indeed cancer.
Dr. Fan told me that for cases like Mr. Liu, patients would first get argon-helium targeted ablation treatment to minimize the size of the tumor, followed by chemotherapy and radiotherapy.
“But, if his budget is very tight, he can go directly to chemotherapy and radiotherapy,” Dr. Fan added.
Mr. Liu accepted Dr. Fan's suggestion and started chemotherapy.
One evening, I stopped by Mr. Liu's ward.
At the entrance, I saw a bed added in the corridor outside of his room.
A middle-aged male patient was cutting meat on his bedside tray with a small knife, and there was a small pan on the lower shelf of the bedside tray.
Next to him, on the floor was an electric stove, ready for cooking.
I was quite surprised by the scene.
Turning left and entering Mr. Liu's room, I saw him lying on the bed and reading a newspaper.
Like the patient I just saw, his wife was also cooking.
After a warm greeting, I first asked him whether he was still smoking.
“Quit a few days ago,” he answered.
I then asked him whether they were allowed to cook in the ward.
He told me that according to the chief nurse, cooking in the ward could set the hospital on fire, and therefore it was forbidden, but everyone still did it.
Eating in the restaurant 3 times a day every day for the duration of his stay would be too expensive, so cooking was the only affordable alternative.

On the way home, I thought of Mr. Liu, a brave albeit uneducated man who survived so many challenges in life, whose last, largest pleasure and perhaps his most effective way of relieving stress, was the treasured freedom, however self-destructive, to smoke.
One last arterial blood gas for a patient with possible obesity hypoventilation syndrome and I would be done for the day.
I entered after a quick knock, not waiting for a reply.
"I need to do a blood test," I began.
She took her eyes off the TV and looked in my direction.
I knew that look—the one where the patient knows you are just an intern and can be shooed away.
She told me to come back later.
She wanted to finish eating a bag of candy.
I was annoyed at having to waste my time on someone who clearly did not care about her health.
I walked out.
Then it hit me: It's not her; it's me!
She doesn't care because she doesn't know.
I do know, and yet I am standing outside her door waiting for her to finish a bag of candy.
I knocked and walked back into her room.
She looked at me again, impatiently.
I asked if I could talk to her about her condition while she finished eating.
I didn't know how to start.
I felt callous telling a patient that she was obese.
I hid behind medical jargon about hypoventilatiori.
I don't think she understood anything.
I left her with her bag of candy.
It is so difficult to bring this up with an obese patient.
The inpatient admitted with uncontrolled diabetes or hypertension presents a perfect teaching opportunity, but how do you tell someone you met 10 minutes ago that they need to lose weight?
Most people in our county hospital do not know the relationship between being overweight and diabetes or heart disease.
How do you wrap up a 15- to 30-minute interview session and tell the patient that they need to lose weight without insulting them?
I found it much easier in the clinic after a relationship had been established.
But inpatients might not return to clinic.
You may never see them again, unless at a readmission.
And then it will probably be a new team and the same awkwardness, ultimately leading to the issue being avoided—again.
I did a small study of 105 medical patients with a body mass index of 30 kg/m'^ or more who were admitted for uncontrolled diabetes or hypertension.
I found that of the approximately 75 patients who smoked, almost 95% were getting some sort of counseling to quit smoking.
However, weight loss was being addressed in only 19%.
Asking a 65-year-old patient admitted for an unrelated condition how much she smoked should be just as awkward.
But it isn't.
It isn't awkward for the patient or the interviewer because it's routine.
The patient knows to expect the question, and the doctor knows that the patient knows.
But how do you ask the patient if they know they are . ..
Another barrier to this discussion is our own attitudes.
Studies about physician perceptions document that primary care physicians have negative attitudes toward their obese patients and think of them as nonadherent and unengaged.
We are convinced that we will not make a difference in these patients' behaviors and outcomes, even before O N BEING A DOCTOR we have tried.

This is especially disturbing, because there is evidence to suggest that patients who do receive weight loss advice are more likely to attempt to lose weight than those who do not receive such counseling.
I have made it routine to ask at the end of the review of symptoms whether there has been any recent change in weight.
This is an innocuous enough question but brings up the issue of weight and in some way can prepare the patient for what follows.
If the patient can recognize an increase in weight, then the job is half done—at least there is awareness.
Most often, this is not the case.
Then my next question is, "Aside from your (chief symptom), what concerns you most about your health?"
On occasion, the answer is about a cancer that "runs in the family" or heart disease.
In that case, obesity can easily be woven in.
However, the most common response is, "Nothing.
I am just worried about (chief symptom)."
This is when I tell them that I would like to talk about my concerns about their health.
"I am concerned that you may develop diabetes or hypertension or even have a stroke in the near future.
There are some risk factors that are not modifiable, like age and family history.
But smoking and obesity are the two most important modifiable factors."
It is easier to state facts rather than opinions.
The most common response is, "Oh!
I have tried everything!"
followed by, "It runs in our family."

Even if the conversation does not go much further, at least it has been mentioned, at least there has been some sort of acknowledgment ofthe health hazards of being overweight, by both the physician and the patient.
This conversation may not always take place during the first interview, but it can definitely occur at some point before discharge.
The American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists recently declared obesity a disease state, recognizing that it is not just a condition.
It is the second leading cause of preventable death in the United States, with an estimated $147 billion in associated medical costs per year.
I think that is a compelling enough reason for us to start mentioning it in our problem lists and addressing it in whatever way possible.
It is important for patients to hear it from all of us, at every visit.
They need to be expecting it at every encounter.
Raising awareness is really the only tool we have at the moment to confront this epidemic.
One of my patients thought that eating a bag of chips after eating candy balances the sweet with the salt.
This was her idea of a balanced diet.
I am sure she is not alone.
It's time we helped them.
In the spring of 2010, my family prepared to move so that my husband could begin a residency program in New York City, about 250 miles from our home in Virginia.
My husband was excited.
He'd matched at an excellent hospital in Manhattan and was eager to begin his program.
I was apprehensive.
I loved where we lived in Virginia and was dreading leaving it behind.
Nonetheless, on an April day, I found myself with a real estate agent, looking at places to rent just outside the city, in New Jersey.
He'd found one for us, a little house, right near the Hudson River.
He brought me down to the water's edge so that I could see the view.
Along the river bank, jagged rocks lined the narrow shore.
The stones were muted shades of rusty gray and hairy with dripping brown algae.
Trash lay tangled between them, old bottles and plastic bags.
The debris thudded rhythmically against the rocks as small waves washed in over them.
A quick breeze carried in the scents of brackish water—hints of brine, mud, and organic decay.
The only movement on the clouded surface of the river were the riffles of competing currents.
Otherwise, the wide breadth of the Hudson was flat and empty.
"Great view, isn't it?"
the realtor asked enthusiastically.
I only nodded.
Directly across the water from us was the hospital where my husband would soon be working.
Behind us, just out of sight, my husband was playing with our 2-year-old daughter.
"You can bring her down here to feed the ducks in the summer," our real estate agent said with a grin.
But again, I could only nod.
I turned away a little and stared down the river at New York's skyline.
The Empire State Building was a sharp point against the crisp, blue sky.
The city's skyline was at once totally recognizable from movies and television shows I had seen.
But it was completely foreign to me as a place to call home.
The city's famous bustle, even invisible from here, felt utterly intimidating.
A sharp gust suddenly blew in off of the river, making the waves rush in against the rocks.
Across the water, an American flag stretched and snapped in the wind on top of one of the hospital buildings.
I stared at the hospital, and for a moment, tried to imagine all the doctors inside caring for patients.
But I failed.
I myself was not a doctor.
The road to becoming a physician is a grueling one.
But in the end, doctors experience the reward of knowing they have healed someone, or eased their pain.
The family members of physicians never experience such satisfaction.
Yet they still share in many of the sacrifices the medical profession exacts from its doctors—including moving to new and unfamiliar places.
The nature of medical education, from the medical school application process to the residency match program, is highly competitive.
It often requires young physicians to uproot themselves—and their families—if they want to pursue training at the best possible hospitals and programs.
In fact, this was our third move to a new state since my husband began his medical career.
So many moves left me feeling as though I were living an unsettled life, a life of constant change.
Overhead, two birds darted by, calling raucously.
Their bodies were a startling jewel green.
"Those are our wild parrots," the real estate agent explained to me.
I asked incredulously.
"In New Jersey?"
"Yep," he said.
"A crate of them supposedly burst open at JFK airport back in the 60s, and they settled here."
I watched the birds, waylaid travelers who'd been misplaced so far from their tropical origins.
They had made their home here for decades, I realized, and they had thrived.
I followed the birds' flight until they disappeared into some nearby trees to my left.
Just up the river, the George Washington Bridge stretched long and silver over the water.
Cars flashed across it, their motion a constant flow into the city that never slept.
And for a moment.
New York City seemed not so intimidating as exhilarating.
Behind me, my daughter came running up.
She dashed past me and then paused near the edge of the water.
She bent, scooped up a handflil of gravel, and flung it into the waves.
Glee showed on her face at all the tiny splashes.
Our realtor turned to me again.
"She's probably never been so close to a river before," he said.
"No," I admitted.
And I smiled for the first time at the smile on her face.
Near the river's edge, my husband knelt down beside my daughter.
Their heads bent toward each other as he spoke to her in words I could not hear.
He pointed to a huge limb of driftwood, tangled in the rocks below.
Together in the air, their fingers traced the twisting shape of the aged wood.
I watched them, freezing the moment in my mind, a moment that I would never have experienced if life, and medicine, had not brought us to this place.
Out on the river, the sun set a patch of the water alight.
It glowed so brilliantly, so beautifully white that it hurt the eyes.
And I felt acutely then that change was a blade that could cut two ways.
This was life: uncertainty and sacrifice, thrill and fresh opportunity.
"The river's never the same," our real estate agent said then.
"It's never the same."
In my final months of residency, I was summoned to see an angry patient.
Mr. R. was furious that our pharmacy did not stock his brand of insulin.
He wanted to issue a complaint.
“You guys always mess up my insulin whenever I am here.
I told the other doctor, and now I'm telling you.
You guys just can't get it right.”
“I'm sorry,” I told him.
“If you prefer, your family can bring your insulin from home and our nurses can administer it.
Would that be an acceptable solution?”
“You people are so incompetent.”
Uncertain of how I might best diffuse the situation, I looked uncomfortably in the direction of my patient's son, who was seated at the bedside.
“You look at me when I talk to you,” Mr. R. commanded.
“Don't you look at him.”
Why don't I come back later?”
As I uncomfortably walked out of the room, he launched a grenade.
“Why don't you go back to India!”
On pure instinct, I responded, “Why don't you leave our [expletive] hospital?”
To underscore my point, I repeated myself.
I exited the room in a cold sweat.
Much of our clinical training focuses on how to modulate our personal style to accommodate patients.
We take doctoring courses that urge compassion, empathy, and cultural sensitivity.
We undergo objective, structured clinical examinations that certify our interpersonal skills.
Our preceptors advise us on subtle techniques and gestures to ensure that patients feel safe, secure, and confident in our care.
Yet, as I reflected on what happened that night, I realized that no one had ever raised the possibility that I might one day be hurt by a patient's words or actions.
What are our obligations when we are the subject of their inhumanity, cruelty, or intolerance?
When the patients whom we are treating fail to express the same decency that they demand?
The prevailing sentiment is that we are supposed to be “better” than our patients.
We are supposed to be able to ignore unpleasant commentary, maintain aplomb, intellectualize difficult situations, and understand the roots of their discontent.
This view was reinforced by one of my colleagues who was taking call with me that night.
In his eyes, I had clearly wronged, and I might consider apologizing to the patient.
“Don't they teach us not to do that?
You're better than that,” he scolded when I shared my story.
“You have to learn to ignore that stuff and rise above it.”
He expressed concern that the patient might report me to our hospital's patient relations committee and that I would be found guilty of some kind of clinical misconduct.
Another colleague was ready to fast-forward through my upset feelings and tried to make light of the fact that I, indeed, had a forthcoming trip to my ancestral homeland.
“It is kind of funny, if you think of it that way.”
But the reality was that I was not above reacting to Mr. R.'s contempt for me, nor did I feel like humor would help me to move on from the situation.
When Mr. R. stopped seeing me as his physician or caregiver, but instead as a foreign face, I was no longer a proud physician at the hospital where I was training.
Instead, I was reduced to a passive subject of a xenophobe's abuse.
After years of feeling that my race was a nonissue, I was subjected to the same kind of hurtful name-calling that I faced in childhood.
Even as self-loathing for not having thicker skin began to creep in, I decided that, on this occasion, my feelings would count.

The following morning, I spoke to my supervising attending physician and absolved myself of future interactions with Mr. R. He and the intern on service would sort out the patient's care without my input.

After rounding on our other patients that morning, I left the hospital with a surprising new sense that, even as I had chosen a profession that calls on me to serve, there are clear limits to that service that I am unwilling to compromise.
I walk into hospital rooms every day, but this time was different.
My father was in this particular hospital room, trying to recover from total hip arthroplasty.
As physicians, my father and I both knew that surgery is never risk-free, but neither of us anticipated this much postoperative drama.
I took a deep breath before entering, hoping to find him in better spirits and without new complications.
Our welcome hug was difficult with his various wires and tubes in the way.
I had barely settled into the chair when he shared the day's epiphany with me.
